name: El Paso — Pass of the North
body: Welcome to El Paso. The name means 'the pass' — and before you dismiss that as generic geography, consider what it actually meant to the people who named it. This is the only natural gap in a mountain chain that runs nearly 2,000 miles from Chihuahua to Colorado. On either side of this corridor: ridgeline after ridgeline, unpassable. Through it: the Rio Grande, flowing south through a wide flat valley, and a crossing shallow enough to ford on foot. That combination made this spot one of the most consequential pieces of real estate in North America for centuries before a single Anglo-American ever laid eyes on it.

The Spanish reached this crossing in 1581 [1], on the northward scouting expeditions that eventually led to the colonization of New Mexico. They called it El Paso del Río del Norte — the Pass of the River of the North — and it became the southern anchor of El Camino Real de Tierra Adentro, the Royal Road of the Interior [2]. That 1,500-mile supply and trade route connected Mexico City to the colonial capital at Santa Fe, and every wagon, every soldier, every priest, every supply train headed north to New Mexico came through this gap for three centuries.

The Rio Grande has always been the organizing fact of this city. It's the international boundary now, and it marks a hard political line between the United States and Mexico. But it didn't always feel that way, and in some respects it still doesn't. The city of Juárez on the Mexican side has more than a million and a half people. El Paso has roughly 700,000. Together they form the largest binational metropolitan area on the U.S.-Mexico border — a single urban zone split by a river that, in some stretches, you could throw a baseball across.

The pass has been continuously inhabited for at least 10,000 years. Archaic hunter-gatherers, then the Manso people, then the Suma and the Jumano, then Spanish colonists and their Pueblo allies fleeing the 1680 revolt, then Mexican settlers and American traders moving along the Chihuahua Trail — everyone who needed to get through the mountains came through here. The city you're driving through right now is the geological product of that constraint. The mountains didn't give people a choice.

We'll have several stops as we work through El Paso. The Mission Trail to the east follows the Rio Grande through some of the oldest continuously inhabited communities in Texas. Take a look at the Franklin Mountains as you drive — that long wall of rock rising directly out of the city is the southern tip of the Rockies, one end of the mountain chain that forced this crossing into existence.

sources.text: El Camino Real de Tierra Adentro — Wikipedia
El Camino Real de Tierra Adentro National Historic Trail — NPS

name: Franklin Mountains & the El Paso Star
body: The mountain range bisecting the city around you is the Franklin Mountains — a 23-mile-long north-south range that runs straight through the middle of El Paso, making it the only major mountain range in the United States entirely contained within a single city. Franklin Mountains State Park protects about 37,000 acres of the range within city limits and is the largest urban wilderness park in the continental United States. [1]

Geologically, the Franklins are the southern tip of the Rocky Mountain chain — the same continental uplift that begins in British Columbia and runs the length of Montana, Colorado, and New Mexico ends here. The rock at the summit is Precambrian granite more than 1.2 billion years old, thrust upward through younger sedimentary layers by faulting along the Rio Grande Rift. The notch you can see cutting through the ridgeline is the Transmountain Road — US-375 — which crosses the range at about 5,300 feet and connects the east side of El Paso to the west. [1]

The large white star on the western flank of the range is the El Paso Star — a 459-foot-wide star made of white-painted rocks laid out by local schoolchildren and community volunteers in 1940. [2] It is illuminated during holidays and for special occasions, visible from the valley floor miles away. For generations of El Paso residents it has been the visual anchor of home — the thing on the mountain that tells you which way you're facing.

The Franklins change color through the day in ways that make them a slow clock for the city below. Red at sunrise, gray by midday, copper and pink in the late afternoon. On the return leg, pay attention to the light on the range as you approach from the north — the granite catches the low angle differently at each hour, and the mountain that looked featureless at noon turns dimensional at dusk.

sources.text: Franklin Mountains State Park — Texas Parks & Wildlife Department
El Paso Star: history and dimensions — El Paso Times archive

name: El Chamizal National Memorial
body: The park you're passing is El Chamizal National Memorial, and it commemorates one of the stranger diplomatic achievements in American history — a land dispute that festered for nearly a century and was finally resolved not by war or courts but by moving a river.

Here's what happened. The Rio Grande is not a stable boundary. It floods, it shifts, it migrates. In 1864, a particularly violent flood cut a new channel for the river, shifting it southward and leaving a tract of land — the chamizal, named for the chamisa shrubs that grew there — suddenly on the American side of where the river used to be [1]. Mexico said it was still Mexican territory; the United States said the boundary followed the new channel. Both sides had defensible legal positions, and neither was willing to back down.

The dispute dragged on for 99 years. Arbitration in 1911 awarded most of the chamizal to Mexico, but the United States refused to accept the ruling [1]. The land sat in legal limbo through two world wars, Korea, and the early Cold War — a grievance right in the middle of an international city.

What finally resolved it was a combination of Cold War politics and personal diplomacy. In 1963, President Kennedy — motivated in part by the desire to keep Mexico from drifting toward Cuba's sphere — proposed a compromise: the United States would cede most of the disputed land to Mexico, and the river channel would be permanently stabilized with concrete to prevent future shifts [2]. Kennedy was assassinated five months later, but the Chamizal Convention was ratified in 1964 under President Johnson. The United States returned 437 acres to Mexico [3], roughly 5,600 El Paso residents whose homes sat on the chamizal were relocated, and the Rio Grande was rechanneled and lined with concrete.

The American side of the former disputed zone became this park — a deliberate act of commemoration for a dispute that ended by doing the right thing, if about a century late. The Mexican side became Parque Chamizal in Juárez.

It's a small park, and easy to drive past without registering its significance. But the Chamizal settlement was genuinely unusual in the history of U.S.-Mexico relations — a case where the United States acknowledged a wrong, gave land back, and got a lasting improvement in bilateral relations in return.

sources.text: Chamizal dispute — Wikipedia
Negotiating the Mexican-American Border: the Case of Chamizal — ADST
The Chamizal Convention — IBWC

name: Concordia Cemetery
body: Coming up on your left is Concordia Cemetery — and if any patch of ground in El Paso tells the full, unfiltered story of this city, it's this one.

Concordia has been a burial ground since the 1850s, and what makes it extraordinary is the sheer range of people interred here. This is not a cemetery that segregated its dead by one criterion. It segregated them by nearly every criterion imaginable — there are separate sections for Catholics and Protestants, for Black and white residents, for Chinese laborers, for Confederate soldiers, for Jewish settlers, and for the unnamed poor. [1]

The most notorious resident is John Wesley Hardin, one of the deadliest gunfighters of the American West, who killed at least 27 men by his own count. Hardin was shot in the back of the head at the Acme Saloon in 1895. He was buried in Concordia. [2]

But the Buffalo Soldiers buried here deserve equal attention. These were African American soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry, established by Congress after the Civil War, who served on the frontier under conditions of extraordinary hardship — fighting both the harsh terrain and the institutionalized racism of the Army they served. [3] The nickname 'Buffalo Soldiers' was given to them by the Plains tribes they sometimes fought — a name the soldiers themselves embraced with pride.

The Chinese section holds the remains of laborers who came to build the railroads and stayed to build businesses in El Paso's early Chinatown — only to face the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882, which barred further immigration and stripped existing residents of the right to citizenship. [4] Many of these graves are unmarked. The families who might have maintained them were dispersed or expelled.

Concordia is a place worth lingering over. In one square plot of desert ground, it holds the tensions and contradictions of the American West — its violence, its ambition, its injustice, and its improbable community.

sources.text: Concordia Cemetery: history and sections — City of El Paso
John Wesley Hardin — Wikipedia
Buffalo Soldiers: 9th and 10th Cavalry — National Archives
Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882 — Library of Congress

name: The Mission Trail — Three Centuries of Adobe
body: You're entering the Mission Trail — and it has a claim worth stating at the start. The three missions along this nine-mile corridor were founded in 1682, making this the oldest mission trail in the United States. The Pilgrims landed at Plymouth in 1620. Jamestown was 1607. The missions on the El Paso Mission Trail predate both the Mayflower and Massachusetts Bay Colony as active communities — and they're still active today. [1]

The trail exists because of catastrophe. The Pueblo Revolt of 1680 drove the entire Spanish colonial population of New Mexico south in a mass retreat — roughly 2,000 colonists and several hundred Pueblo peoples who had aligned with or been brought along by the Spanish fled through the Jornada del Muerto and gathered in the El Paso valley. Among them were Tiwa-speaking people from Isleta Pueblo, Piro people from the Rio Grande villages, and others who would never return north. They established Ysleta, Socorro, and San Elizario along this stretch of the river in the years immediately following the revolt. [2]

What is remarkable about the Mission Trail is not just its age but its continuity. These communities have not been restored or reconstructed — they have been in continuous use. Ysleta Mission has had an active parish since 1682. Socorro Mission has served the same community without interruption for more than three centuries. [3] The people buried in these churchyards are the ancestors of people who live in these communities today. That's not a historical claim. It's a present-tense fact.

The missions are about three miles apart, following the old road south along the river. Ysleta is the first and the oldest. Take your time between them — this stretch of the Rio Grande valley is one of the quietest places on the trip.

sources.text: Mission Trail, El Paso — El Paso Museum of History
Pueblo Revolt 1680: Spanish retreat and mission founding — NPS El Camino Real
Ysleta del Sur Pueblo and Socorro Mission history — YDSP official

name: Ysleta del Sur Pueblo & Tigua Mission
body: The community you're entering now is Ysleta del Sur Pueblo, and its history begins not here but 700 miles north — at a place called Isleta Pueblo, in the middle of the Rio Grande valley near modern Albuquerque.

When the Pueblo Revolt of 1680 drove the Spanish colonists out of New Mexico, Governor Antonio de Otermín fled south with approximately 2,000 colonists and several hundred Pueblo people who had sided with the Spanish, or who feared retribution, or who simply had no other choice. Among those who made the journey south were Tiwa-speaking people from Isleta Pueblo. They settled here at Ysleta — a corruption of 'Isleta,' meaning 'little island' — and established the community in 1682 [1]. They have never left.

Ysleta del Sur Pueblo is the oldest continuously occupied community in Texas [2], predating American independence by nearly a century. The Tigua people maintained their identity, their ceremonial traditions, and their tribal governance through three and a half centuries of Spanish colonial rule, Mexican sovereignty, American annexation, and the grinding pressures of the 20th century.

The mission church adjacent to the pueblo — the Ysleta Mission, recognized as the oldest continuously operating parish in Texas [2] — was originally founded in 1682, though it has been rebuilt several times after floods and fires. The distinctive white adobe walls and bell tower are characteristic of the Spanish mission architecture that runs along this stretch of the river.

The Tigua story has a painful chapter in the 20th century. Texas refused to recognize the tribe as an Indian nation for decades, and it wasn't until 1987 that Congress passed the Ysleta del Sur Pueblo Restoration Act, formally restoring their federal status [1] and access to the health, education, and housing programs available to other sovereign tribal nations.

Today the Pueblo operates a cultural center and is deeply involved in language and ceremonial revitalization — working to preserve Tiwa traditions that survived three centuries precisely because the community refused to let them go.

sources.text: Ysleta del Sur Pueblo — Wikipedia
Ysleta del Sur Pueblo Cultural Center — YDSP Official Site

name: Socorro Mission
body: Coming up is the Socorro Mission — Nuestra Señora de la Concepción del Socorro — and like Ysleta just to the northwest, its founding is inseparable from the catastrophe of the 1680 Pueblo Revolt.

The Piro people were among the southernmost of the Pueblo nations, living along the Rio Grande in villages that occupied the area now covered by the Elephant Butte reservoir and the Jornada del Muerto region we'll pass later. When the revolt drove the Spanish from New Mexico, a substantial group of Piro people — along with some Manso and Suma — fled south with the colonists. They established a community here at Socorro in 1682, the same year as Ysleta [1].

The word 'socorro' means aid or assistance — a name that reflected the community's role as a way station and resupply point on El Camino Real. The mission church was built to serve the displaced Piro community and the Spanish settlers who gathered here in the years between the revolt and the eventual reconquest of New Mexico in 1692.

The present church structure dates largely from the mid-19th century, though it stands on the foundations of the earlier colonial building and maintains the basic form of Spanish mission architecture: thick adobe walls, deep-set windows, a single bell tower, and an orientation toward the east so that the rising sun illuminates the altar at dawn.

The Piro as a distinct people largely disappeared from the historical record by the 18th century — absorbed through intermarriage with Spanish colonists and neighboring communities, or dispersed, or simply unrecorded. But the mission they helped build has stood continuously for more than three centuries, still serving an active Catholic parish in a village that has never stopped being a village. That continuity — of place if not of people — is the quiet fact that the Socorro Mission embodies.

sources.text: El Camino Real de Tierra Adentro — NPS (Pueblo Revolt, mission founding)

name: San Elizario Presidio & the Salt Wars
body: You're now entering San Elizario, and the small, sun-bleached chapel at the center of this village is one of the oldest continually active churches in Texas. The original Spanish presidio — a frontier military garrison — was established here in the late 18th century to protect El Camino Real and the communities of the lower valley. [1]
But San Elizario's most remarkable chapter is a conflict called the El Paso Salt War — one of the strangest, most overlooked episodes in American history.

About 100 miles east of here, near the Guadalupe Mountains, there are vast natural salt lakes that had been used freely by local Hispanic and Pueblo communities for generations. Salt was not a luxury out here — it was essential for preserving meat, curing hides, and trading. These communities considered the salt lakes a commons — shared resources belonging to no single owner.

After the Civil War, Anglo-American entrepreneurs began claiming legal title to the salt lakes, attempting to charge for access to something people had always taken freely. Tensions simmered for years. Then in 1877, they boiled over. A group of several hundred Tejano and Mexican residents — in a remarkable act of cross-border solidarity — besieged and captured a company of Texas Rangers right here in San Elizario, forcing their surrender and freeing prisoners who had been arrested for taking salt. [2]

It was the last armed uprising on Texas soil. The Texas Rangers were humiliated, the ringleaders fled to Mexico, and the salt lakes eventually passed into private hands anyway. But the Salt War left a lasting mark on the borderlands — a reminder that the border communities here had their own legal traditions and their own sense of collective rights that preceded American sovereignty. [3]
San Elizario today is quiet, but stand in that plaza long enough and you can feel the weight of it.

sources.text: San Elizario Presidio Chapel — Texas Historical Commission
El Paso Salt War — Texas State Historical Association
El Paso Salt War — Wikipedia

name: Hueco Tanks — Drive-By
body: About 30 miles to the northeast — in the direction of those low volcanic hills you may be able to see on the horizon — is Hueco Tanks State Park and Historic Site, one of the most remarkable archaeological sites in the American Southwest. We're not stopping today, but it's worth knowing it's there.

Hueco means 'hollow' in Spanish, and the tanks are exactly that: natural rock basins carved into the syenite boulders by thousands of years of erosion, that collect and hold rainwater. In a desert where reliable water is survival, these basins have drawn people for at least 10,000 years [1]. Archaic peoples, Jornada Mogollon, the Mescalero Apache, the Kiowa, the Tigua — all of them came here, and many of them left their marks.

The boulders at Hueco Tanks are covered in petroglyphs and pictographs — paintings and carvings left by people across millennia. There are more than 3,000 images [2]: masks, kachina figures, handprints, animals, symbols whose meanings have been lost and others still understood by the communities whose ancestors made them. The site holds the largest concentration of painted mask designs in North America [1]. Access is carefully controlled: guided tours only, permit required, and certain areas permanently closed to non-tribal visitors out of respect for their ceremonial significance.

If you have an extra day before or after the main trip, Hueco Tanks rewards it. The guided tours are genuinely excellent, the rock art is extraordinary, and the landscape — these rounded volcanic boulders sitting in the flat Chihuahuan Desert, with the Franklin Mountains in the background — is unlike anything else in the region.

sources.text: Hueco Tanks — Wikipedia
Pictograph Tours — Texas Parks & Wildlife Department

name: Fort Bliss & the Tularosa Basin
body: As you leave El Paso heading north on Highway 70, you're entering one of the most consequential and least-visited landscapes in American military history. Fort Bliss — which sprawls across the desert to your right — is the largest installation in the U.S. Army by land area, covering roughly 1.1 million acres of the Chihuahuan Desert. Most of that land is empty. Some of it is not.

Fort Bliss was established in 1848, in the immediate aftermath of the Mexican-American War, as a frontier post to protect the new American territory and the trade routes running through El Paso. It grew slowly through the Indian Wars period — this was Apache country, and the fort played a role in the campaigns that eventually forced Geronimo's surrender in 1886. But its modern significance has almost nothing to do with those 19th-century origins.

In 1945, Fort Bliss became the destination for Operation Paperclip — the classified American program that brought German rocket scientists to the United States after World War II. Wernher von Braun, the lead engineer of the V-2 rocket program, arrived at Fort Bliss in late 1945 along with more than a hundred of his colleagues [1]. They brought their notes, their engineering drawings, and in some cases their partially completed rockets. For five years, von Braun and his team lived and worked at Fort Bliss, conducting test launches at White Sands, developing the technology that would eventually power the American space program.

The ethical dimensions of Paperclip were controversial from the start and remain so. Von Braun was a member of the Nazi Party and held SS rank [2]. His V-2 rockets had killed thousands of civilians in London and Antwerp. The rockets themselves had been built using forced labor from concentration camps — as many as 10,000 prisoners died in the underground Mittelwerk factory producing V-2s [2]. The U.S. government classified this history, altered personnel files, and proceeded with the recruitment anyway, judging that the scientific value outweighed the moral cost.

Von Braun later became the public face of American space exploration — he designed the Saturn V rocket that took Apollo astronauts to the moon, was celebrated on the cover of Time magazine, and worked closely with Disney to popularize the idea of space travel to the American public. His German past was rarely discussed publicly until the 1980s when files were declassified — and has never been fully resolved [2].

The Tularosa Basin you're now driving through is a closed basin — no river flows out of it to the ocean. Water drains in from the surrounding mountains and either evaporates or sinks into the groundwater. This geological isolation is what created White Sands. It's also what made this corridor so valuable to the military: the basin is vast, flat, largely uninhabited, and closed off on all sides by mountain ranges. It is an ideal place to test things you don't want anyone to see.

sources.text: Operation Paperclip at Fort Bliss: 1945–1950 — White Sands Missile Range Museum
Project Paperclip and American Rocketry after World War II — National Air and Space Museum

name: Mescalero Apache & the Tularosa Basin
body: The basin you're crossing has been Mescalero Apache territory for as long as there are records — and for much of the colonial and territorial periods, it was effectively ungovernable by anyone else. The Mescalero name comes from the Spanish: mescalero, 'people of the mescal.' [1] The century plant — the massive agave that grows on the slopes of the Sacramento and Guadalupe Mountains to the east — was the foundation of the Mescalero economy. Whole communities would gather at harvest sites in late spring, dig the hearts of the plants, and roast them for days in large pit ovens until the bitter raw flesh converted to a dense, sweet, calorie-rich food that could be stored and carried. [2] The mountains around you fed people for centuries through this single plant.

The Mescalero ranged across an enormous territory — from the Rio Grande east to the Pecos, from southern Colorado south into Chihuahua — and they used the mountain ranges of southern New Mexico as a strategic system. [1] The Sacramentos, the Guadalupes, the Organs, the San Andres: each range offered water, game, escape routes, and high ground that made pursuit from below nearly impossible. For three centuries, Spanish, Mexican, and American military forces tried to project power through the Tularosa Basin and found themselves consistently outmaneuvered by people who knew the terrain in a way no outsider could match.

The Spanish first documented the Mescalero in the mid-1500s. For three centuries following, the relationship cycled through the same pattern: periods of trade and uneasy coexistence, followed by raiding, followed by punitive campaigns, followed by treaties, followed by raiding again. [3] The Spanish and Mexican governments never established lasting control over the Tularosa Basin. The U.S. Army thought it could do better.

In 1862, with the Civil War raging in the East and Confederate forces having just been expelled from New Mexico, General James Carleton took command of the New Mexico Military Department with a systematic plan. He would subdue each of the region's major Indigenous nations in sequence — starting with the Mescalero in the southeast. His instrument was Colonel Christopher 'Kit' Carson. Carson's campaign was swift: Mescalero leader Cadete surrendered in early 1863 with roughly 400 of his people, and they were force-marched to the Bosque Redondo reservation on the Pecos River. [4]

They were expected to become farmers in alkaline soil that would not support crops, with water sources that made people sick, confined alongside the Navajo — their traditional enemies — who would follow them to the same reservation within the year. [4] The Mescalero lasted less than three years at Bosque Redondo. In November 1865, most of the Mescalero simply disappeared into the night — slipping away from the reservation in small groups, making their way back across 200 miles of hostile territory to the Sacramento Mountains they had never stopped considering home. The Army made no serious attempt to recapture them.

President Grant established the Mescalero Apache Reservation in the Sacramento Mountains in 1873, on a portion of their ancestral territory — roughly 40 miles east of where you're driving right now. [1] The reservation encompasses nearly 720 square miles of the Sacramento Mountains, from high desert up to the 12,000-foot Sierra Blanca peak.

The Mescalero Apache Tribe today has around 4,500 enrolled members and operates the Inn of the Mountain Gods resort and casino near Ruidoso, along with significant timber and cattle operations on the reservation. [1] The sacred practices, the language revitalization programs, and the girlhood ceremony — the four-day coming-of-age rite that is one of the most important ceremonies in Apache culture — continue today on the same mountain land where the Mescalero have always lived.

citations.text: New Mexico Magazine — 100th Anniversary: Mescal Roast
Bosque Redondo Memorial Digital Collections — Ndé (Mescalero Apache)
New Mexico Culture — Bosque Redondo Memorial Commemorates

sources.text: Mescalero Apache Tribe: history — Mescalero Apache Tribe official
Mescalero Apache — Wikipedia
Bosque Redondo Memorial — New Mexico Historic Sites

name: Organ Mountains
body: The mountain range rising sharply to your left is the Organ Mountains — named for the towering vertical spires of granite that line the ridgeline and, from the right angle, look like the pipes of a massive pipe organ. The tallest peak, Organ Needle, tops out at 9,012 feet.

These mountains are geologically distinctive. The Organs are an intrusive igneous formation — magma pushed into existing sedimentary rock without breaking through, then exposed over millions of years as erosion stripped the softer layers away [1]. The result is those spectacular vertical spires — rock that was once miles underground, now standing in open desert air.

The Organ Mountains were Mescalero Apache territory for centuries. The peaks and canyons provided escape routes, water sources, and hunting grounds that the Apache used with devastating effectiveness against Spanish, Mexican, and American forces who tried to project military power through the Tularosa Basin. The labyrinthine terrain of the upper Organs made pursuit almost impossible.

In 2014, President Obama designated the Organ Mountains and a stretch of the adjacent desert as the Organ Mountains-Desert Peaks National Monument — protecting nearly 496,000 acres of Chihuahuan Desert [2]. The designation was strongly supported by local communities who had advocated for it for decades. In 2017, the Trump administration reviewed it for reduction, but it was ultimately left intact.

If you have time on the way back through Las Cruces, the Dripping Springs trail into the foothills gives you a close look at the rock spires and access to a historic resort site — a tuberculosis sanatorium built in the 1870s that exploited the dry mountain air for treatment before antibiotics existed. The ruins are still there, tucked into a canyon below the main ridgeline.

sources.text: Organ Mountains — Wikipedia
President Obama Designates Organ Mountains-Desert Peaks National Monument — White House

name: San Andres Mountains & Trinity Corridor
body: The mountain range you can see to the northwest — longer and lower than the Organs, running roughly parallel to your route — is the San Andres Mountains. They're the source of the gypsum that built White Sands, and they're also, somewhat improbably, one of the most restricted pieces of real estate in the United States.

The San Andres are entirely enclosed within White Sands Missile Range. No public access. No hiking, no roads in, no exceptions. The range has been militarily controlled since 1945, and in that time — paradoxically — it has become one of the most ecologically pristine areas in the Chihuahuan Desert. Without livestock, without off-road vehicles, without development of any kind, the desert ecosystem has remained largely intact for 80 years. Mule deer, pronghorn antelope, and even a remnant population of desert bighorn sheep live in the San Andres in near-total isolation from human interference.

Roughly 30 miles northeast of here, in the valley between the San Andres and the Oscura Mountains, is the Trinity Site — where the first nuclear weapon was detonated at 5:29 a.m. on July 16, 1945 [1]. We'll cover that in more detail when you reach the Jornada del Muerto section, since the detonation site sits within that historic corridor. But as you drive through this landscape, it's worth registering that you're moving through the outer perimeter of what was, in the summer of 1945, the most secret location in the world.

The mountains you're looking at, the flat desert floor, the enormous sky — all of it was the backdrop for a moment that permanently altered the course of human history. The secrecy has mostly lifted since then, but the missile range remains active, and the landscape looks much as it did the morning they counted down to zero.

sources.text: Trinity Site — National Park Service

name: White Sands National Park Entrance
body: Welcome to White Sands — and take a moment before you step out of the car, because what you're about to see is genuinely unlike anything else on Earth.

The white dunes stretching out in every direction are not sand in the usual sense. They're gypsum — calcium sulfate dihydrate — the same mineral used to make drywall and plaster of Paris. And gypsum is remarkable because it's one of the very few minerals soft enough to remain cool to the touch even under direct desert sun — which is why walking barefoot on these dunes in summer is actually possible. [1]

So where does all this gypsum come from? The story begins about 250 million years ago, when this entire region was covered by a shallow inland sea. When that sea evaporated, it left behind enormous deposits of gypsum in the rock of the surrounding mountains — particularly the San Andres range to the west. Rainfall dissolves that gypsum, carries it down into the Tularosa Basin, and because the basin has no outlet to the ocean, the water eventually evaporates and leaves the gypsum behind. The whole system is young by geological standards — perhaps only 7,000 years old. And it's still moving. The dunes migrate northeast at rates up to 30 feet per year. [2]

Life in this environment seems like it should be impossible. And yet — White Sands is one of the most striking examples of rapid evolution you'll find anywhere. The bleached earless lizard, the Apache pocket mouse, the White Sands pupfish — all have developed pale, near-white coloration since the dunes formed, within the last several thousand years. That's a blink of an eye in evolutionary time. [3]

There's a human dimension here too, and a complicated one. White Sands sits entirely within White Sands Missile Range, the largest military installation in the United States by area. The park and the missile range share the land — and several times a year, the park closes entirely for missile tests. [4] That military history runs deep here, and we'll say more about it in a moment when we pass the Trinity Site corridor.

But for now — get out, take your shoes off if you want, and walk a few steps into those dunes. The silence out here, in the middle of this white expanse, is something you don't easily find anywhere else.

sources.text: White Sands National Park: geology — NPS
Gypsum dune formation, Tularosa Basin — NPS White Sands
Rapid evolution of White Sands lizards and mice — University of New Mexico / Science
White Sands Missile Range and national park closures — NPS

name: Las Cruces & the Mesilla Valley
body: You're entering the outskirts of Las Cruces — the second-largest city in New Mexico, with about 115,000 people. The name means 'the crosses,' and it refers to the crosses that marked a massacre site on this plain in the 1830s, when a group of travelers on the Chihuahua Trail was killed by Apache raiders. A later party found the bodies, buried them, and marked the graves with wooden crosses. When American surveyors came through in the 1840s, they named the location after what they saw [1].

That grim founding story fits a place that has been a contested frontier for centuries. The Mesilla Valley — the agricultural corridor along the Rio Grande here — was Mexican territory until 1848, then an ambiguous zone following the Mexican-American War, and then the subject of a final border negotiation called the Gadsden Purchase in 1853, which brought this strip of southern New Mexico and Arizona firmly into the United States. Mexico sold it, under pressure, for $10 million [1].

The reason the U.S. wanted this specific strip of desert had nothing to do with agriculture and everything to do with railroads. The proposed southern transcontinental rail route — which eventually became the Southern Pacific — needed a low-elevation corridor through the mountains. The Mesilla Valley provided it. The Gadsden Purchase is, at its core, a railroad land deal.

New Mexico State University is here — a land-grant institution founded in 1888, with strong programs in agriculture, engineering, and space science. The White Sands connection runs deep: NMSU has had research partnerships with the missile range and related federal programs since the 1940s.

The Rio Grande you can see to the west runs through a rich agricultural valley here — chile peppers, pecans, cotton, onions. The Mesilla Valley produces a significant fraction of New Mexico's agricultural output, and it does it in a landscape that receives only eight inches of rain per year. The river makes all of it possible.

sources.text: Las Cruces, New Mexico — Wikipedia

name: Old Mesilla Plaza
body: A few miles west of Las Cruces, just off the highway, is the old village of Mesilla — and if you haven't stopped already, it's worth a detour. The plaza at the center of Mesilla is one of the best-preserved 19th-century village squares in the American Southwest, and the history layered into it is genuinely extraordinary.

When the Mexican-American War ended in 1848 and the boundary was drawn giving New Mexico to the United States, a group of Mexican residents on the American side of the line chose to move south rather than become American citizens. They founded Mesilla in 1850 on what they believed to be Mexican soil. Then the Gadsden Purchase of 1853 moved the boundary again, and Mesilla found itself in the United States despite having been founded specifically to remain in Mexico. The inhabitants who had moved to stay Mexican were now Americans without having moved an inch [1].

This same plaza was a critical waypoint on the Butterfield Overland Mail route, the 2,800-mile stagecoach line that connected St. Louis to San Francisco between 1858 and 1861. Mesilla sat almost exactly at the midpoint of the route, making it the most substantial stop between Texas and California [2]. The building now occupied by La Posta restaurant — a Mesilla institution for decades — was one of the original Butterfield stations. Waterman Ormsby, a New York Herald reporter who rode the first westbound stage in 1858, described Mesilla as the largest and most prosperous settlement he'd seen since leaving Texas. The route closed when the Civil War brought Confederate forces into the region in 1861, cutting off the southern mail corridor.

During the Civil War, Mesilla briefly served as the capital of the Confederate Territory of Arizona — a Confederate claim to the southern portions of New Mexico and Arizona that lasted from 1861 until Union forces retook the region in 1862. The Confederate flag flew over this plaza for about six months.

One more layer: Mesilla was the most important station on the entire Butterfield Overland Mail route — the 2,795-mile stagecoach line that ran from St. Louis to San Francisco from 1858 to 1861. [1] Mesilla sat almost exactly at the midpoint of the route, and it was here that the stagecoach crossed the Rio Grande and turned west toward California. The building that now houses La Posta restaurant, on the south side of the plaza, was the original Butterfield station — a large adobe structure with corrals that could process horses and passengers before the long push through the Sonoran Desert. Reporter Waterman Ormsby, who was the only through-passenger on the very first westbound run in September 1858 and filed dispatches to the New York Herald as he traveled, described Mesilla as the most substantial stop between Texas and California. [2] The line operated for just under three years before the Civil War forced it to close — the southern route ran through Confederate-claimed territory — and the transcontinental stagecoach era effectively ended with it. [1]
CITATION_BLOCK_13

The most famous person associated with Mesilla is Billy the Kid, who was tried here in 1881 for the murder of a sheriff and sentenced to death by hanging. He escaped from the Lincoln County Courthouse before the sentence was carried out — killing two guards in the process — and was shot by Sheriff Pat Garrett two months later at Fort Sumner, 150 miles northeast of here.

The plaza today has a functioning church from 1851, a scattering of good restaurants and galleries, and the kind of afternoon quiet that makes it easy to forget you're half a mile from a major highway. The San Albino Basilica on the north side of the square is worth stepping into — the building has been in continuous use for over 170 years.

sources.text: Mesilla, New Mexico — Wikipedia
Butterfield Overland Mail — Texas State Historical Association
Texas State Historical Association — Butterfield Overland Mail
Butterfield Overland Mail Route — The First Running of the Stage

name: Hatch — Chile Capital of the World
body: You're coming into Hatch, population around 1,600, which has declared itself the Chile Capital of the World — and while that's a marketing tagline, it rests on a genuine agricultural tradition.

The Hatch Valley has been growing chile peppers along the Rio Grande for over a century, and the combination of long summer days, cool nights, fertile alluvial soil, and irrigation from the river produces a chile pepper with a flavor profile that agricultural scientists confirm is genuinely distinct from chiles grown elsewhere [1]. The soil chemistry, the temperature differential between day and night, and the particular varieties developed here — including the NuMex Big Jim, bred at New Mexico State University in 1975 and still one of the most-planted varieties — all contribute to a flavor that chile enthusiasts consider superior to commercially grown alternatives [2].

New Mexico takes its chile seriously in a way that can surprise outsiders. There is a state question — 'red or green?' — that refers to which chile sauce you want on your food, and the correct answer in New Mexico is almost always 'Christmas,' meaning both. Green chile is the fresh-roasted version, earthy and bright. Red chile is the dried and reconstituted version, darker and more complex. The two are not interchangeable, and New Mexicans have strong opinions about which dishes call for which.

Late August and September are the roasting season in Hatch, when the air fills with the smell of chiles roasting in wire cages over propane burners — a smell that, for people who grew up in New Mexico, is as evocative of home as almost anything. The Hatch Chile Festival, held on Labor Day weekend, draws tens of thousands of people to this tiny town every year [1].

If you want to bring some back, the roadside stands through Hatch are the real thing. Buy fresh-roasted if it's the season; buy dried ristras or frozen if it's not. The frozen roasted green chile from Hatch, vacuum-packed, travels reasonably well and is the closest thing to the real experience you can get outside the valley.

sources.text: Hatch, New Mexico Is for Chile Lovers — New Mexico Magazine
What is Hatch Chile? — Hatch Chile Store

name: Caballo Lake
body: The reservoir on your left is Caballo Lake — 'horse lake' in Spanish — one of a chain of reservoirs along the Rio Grande built in the first half of the 20th century as part of the Rio Grande Project, an ambitious federal water management system designed to tame a river that had been flooding, silting, and shifting course since long before humans tried to farm along it.

Caballo was completed in 1938, about 25 years after the larger Elephant Butte Dam upstream [1]. Its primary function is not water storage but regulation — it receives water released from Elephant Butte and holds it until it can be delivered to the irrigation districts downstream in New Mexico and Texas and, by treaty, to Mexico. The two reservoirs work as a system: Elephant Butte stores the long-term water supply; Caballo manages the daily and seasonal releases.

The Rio Grande Project transformed this valley. Before the dams, the river flooded unpredictably every spring with snowmelt from the Colorado Rockies, depositing sediment and then pulling back to a trickle by late summer. Farming was possible but precarious. With the dams, farmers in the Mesilla Valley and El Paso valley could count on reliable water deliveries — and the agricultural economy of southern New Mexico was built on that reliability [1].

The lake is a popular spot for winter camping and fishing. Because it sits lower and warmer than the surrounding high desert, snowbirds and retirees from the northern states have been coming here for decades with campers and boats. The RV parks along the shore have a certain timeless quality — the same trucks, the same awnings, the same lawn chairs — that feels like it hasn't changed much since the 1970s.

sources.text: Rio Grande Project — U.S. Bureau of Reclamation

name: Elephant Butte Lake
body: Coming up on your right is Elephant Butte Lake — New Mexico's largest body of water, created by the Elephant Butte Dam across the Rio Grande. When it was completed in 1916, Elephant Butte was the largest dam in the United States and its reservoir was the largest man-made lake in the world [1]. It held that distinction for decades, which gives you a sense of how aggressively the early 20th century went after the engineering challenges of the American West.

The name comes from the volcanic butte visible from the water — a lava plug eroded into a shape that, from certain angles, resembles an elephant. The butte was well above the water line when the dam was built, but as the reservoir filled, the water rose around it and it became an island. That island is still there, visible from the shore.

The dam was a federal project authorized under the Reclamation Act of 1902 — the foundational law that gave the federal government authority to build water infrastructure throughout the arid West [2]. The Reclamation Act was not a neutral piece of engineering; it was a deliberate policy of transforming desert into farmland, enabling settlement, and incorporating the West into the national agricultural economy. The dams it authorized drowned canyons, displaced communities, and altered river ecosystems in ways that are still being reckoned with.

Below the water of Elephant Butte Lake lie the ruins of a town called Palomas — a village that was inundated when the reservoir filled and was simply left in place. On exceptionally low-water years, the ruins become partially visible. The lake also covers portions of the prehistoric Piro Pueblo sites along the Rio Grande, as well as segments of El Camino Real that ran along the riverbank.

The lake is heavily used for recreation — boating, fishing, water skiing — and the little town of Elephant Butte, just north of here, exists almost entirely to serve that economy. It's a cheerful, slightly ramshackle resort community of the kind the Southwest produces in abundance.

sources.text: Elephant Butte Reservoir — Wikipedia
Rio Grande Project — NPS Teaching with Historic Places

name: Truth or Consequences
body: You've arrived in Truth or Consequences — and yes, that is actually the city's name, and yes, it was changed from 'Hot Springs' in 1950 to win a radio contest.

The NBC radio program 'Truth or Consequences' — a game show hosted by Ralph Edwards — offered to broadcast its tenth anniversary episode from any city in the United States that would rename itself after the show. Hot Springs, New Mexico was the only city to actually do it, winning the contest and the publicity, and has lived with the name ever since [1]. Ralph Edwards returned to the city every year for the Truth or Consequences Fiesta until his death in 2005.

The original name — Hot Springs — was accurate. The city sits atop a geothermal field fed by the Rio Grande Rift, the same tectonic feature that runs the length of New Mexico. Naturally heated water at temperatures ranging from 98 to 115 degrees Fahrenheit bubbles up throughout the downtown area, and the city was a therapeutic destination long before the name change. The Mescalero Apache used the springs for centuries; Spanish travelers documented them in the colonial period; and by the early 20th century, Hot Springs had developed into a regional health resort.

The spa culture never entirely went away. Truth or Consequences has a cluster of small bathhouses in the downtown area where you can soak in private mineral tubs for modest fees — not the elaborate European-style spa facilities you'd find at a resort, but clean, unpretentious, genuinely therapeutic hot water that has been coming out of the ground here for as long as anyone has records.

The city has attracted a modest wave of artists and retirees in recent years, drawn by cheap property values, the hot springs, and the particular New Mexico quality of being left alone. The downtown has a handful of good restaurants, an art gallery or two, and a lot of empty storefronts. It's the kind of place that rewards wandering.

sources.text: Truth or Consequences, New Mexico — Wikipedia

name: Spaceport America
body: About 30 miles to the southwest — invisible from I-25 but close enough that it registers as more than an abstraction — is Spaceport America, the world's first purpose-built commercial spaceport. And its history is a useful case study in the gap between futurist optimism and the actual pace of change.

The state of New Mexico began developing the concept in the early 2000s, betting that commercial space tourism was imminent and that a remote desert corridor with restricted airspace, high altitude, and clear skies was a natural home for a launch facility. New Mexico voters approved a gross receipts tax in Sierra County and Doña Ana County to help fund construction [1].

The anchor tenant was Virgin Galactic, Richard Branson's space tourism venture, which signed a long-term lease and gave the project its initial momentum. The terminal building — a striking curved structure designed by the British firm Foster + Partners, partially buried in the desert to reduce its visual impact — was completed in 2011 at a cost of about $209 million, most of it public money [1].

What followed was a long and often frustrating series of delays. Virgin Galactic's SpaceShipTwo development program was set back severely by a fatal test flight accident in 2014 that killed the co-pilot and seriously injured the pilot over the Mojave Desert. The investigation found that the co-pilot had unlocked the vehicle's feathering system too early during descent, but also identified design vulnerabilities that contributed to the accident [2]. The program was redesigned, rebuilt, and eventually resumed.

Virgin Galactic completed several commercial flights from Spaceport America in 2023, charging ticket prices around $450,000 per seat for a roughly 90-minute experience that includes a few minutes of weightlessness and views of the Earth's curvature. The company then suspended flights to focus on developing its next-generation spacecraft and announced it would shift operations, leaving the spaceport without its anchor tenant [1].

The spaceport does tours, and the Foster + Partners building is architecturally interesting — if you have an extra afternoon, it's about a 45-minute drive from T or C. But the larger story it tells is about how hard it is to make genuinely new industries work on a predicted timeline, even when the vision is real and the infrastructure is built.

sources.text: Spaceport America — Wikipedia
Virgin Galactic SpaceShipTwo accident — NTSB investigation summary

name: Jornada del Muerto
body: The stretch of high desert you're moving through right now has one of the most evocative names in North American geography: the Jornada del Muerto. The Journey of the Dead Man.

It refers to a 90-mile corridor of waterless desert running roughly north from here toward Socorro — the same basic direction you're traveling on I-25. For three centuries, this was the most feared section of El Camino Real, the royal road connecting Mexico City to the colonial settlements of New Mexico. [1]

The problem was simple and brutal: 90 miles with no reliable water source. In a landscape where June temperatures routinely hit 105 degrees, where flash floods could appear from nowhere, where the scrub and the stone offered no shade, travelers had to carry enough water to get through or die trying.

The trail gets its name from a German merchant named Bernardo Gruber, who escaped from a New Mexico Inquisition prison in Santa Fe in 1670, fled south, and died somewhere in this desert before reaching safety. His bleached bones were found by later travelers. [2]

The Spanish colonial traffic on this road was staggering. Supply caravans — called the situado — traveled north from Mexico City every three years, hauling tools, cloth, seeds, and religious articles for the missions and settlements of New Mexico. The return journey carried hides, salt, and piñón. Every convoy was a gamble against heat, drought, and Apache raiders who knew this corridor intimately and used Spanish reliance on the route to devastating effect. [3]

The 1680 Pueblo Revolt sent the entire Spanish colonial population of New Mexico fleeing south through this desert. Around 2,000 Spanish colonists and their Pueblo allies, many of them wounded, many on foot, crossed the Jornada in retreat — one of the most harrowing forced marches in North American history. [4]

The American Civil War brought the Jornada another chapter. Confederate forces under General Henry Sibley crossed it in 1862 as part of a plan to capture the Southwest for the Confederacy. The plan failed at the Battle of Glorieta Pass, further north, and Sibley's army retreated back through this same desert, starving. [5]

And then, of course, there is Trinity. On July 16, 1945, at 5:29 in the morning, the first nuclear weapon ever detonated exploded at a site within the Jornada del Muerto — about 30 miles east of the modern highway. The explosion released energy equivalent to 21 kilotons of TNT, vaporized the steel tower holding the device, and fused the desert sand into a glassy mineral now called trinitite. [6]

The Trinity Site is open to the public twice a year — the first Saturday in April and the first Saturday in October. Just a low obelisk in the desert, a patch of trinitite behind a chain-link fence, and the enormous sky. You'll have a while yet before Socorro. The desert out there doesn't look like much from the highway. But there is almost no ground in North America that has been crossed by more people, for more centuries, with higher stakes than what you're looking at right now.

Jornada del Muerto: name and history — Wikipedia

Pueblo Revolt 1680: Spanish retreat south — Wikipedia
Battle of Glorieta Pass and Sibley's campaign — Wikipedia

name: Socorro, New Mexico
body: You're coming into Socorro — a name that means 'aid' or 'help' in Spanish, for the same reason as the Socorro Mission on the Mission Trail: this stretch of the Rio Grande was a relief and resupply point for travelers who had just crossed the Jornada del Muerto to the south, or who were about to face it going north.

Socorro is home to New Mexico Tech — the New Mexico Institute of Mining and Technology — one of the better small technical universities in the American West. It's particularly known for its programs in geophysics, hydrology, and explosives engineering. New Mexico Tech's Energetic Materials Research and Testing Center is one of the few places in the country that does federally sanctioned research on explosives and their effects [1].

The town itself is older than the university. Spanish colonists established a mission at the Pilabó Pueblo here in 1627, and the present church — San Miguel Mission — sits on the foundations of that colonial structure, with walls dating from the 17th century. The church is one of the oldest in New Mexico and is still an active parish [1].

The flatlands east of Socorro mark the beginning of the routes used during the Navajo Long Walk of 1864 — a story we'll tell at the next stop. You're standing at the edge of one of the darkest chapters in New Mexico history.

The flatlands east of the Rio Grande here were also the beginning of the long route to Bosque Redondo — the notorious reservation on the Pecos River where the U.S. Army imprisoned the Navajo and Mescalero Apache beginning in 1863. The forced marches, known to the Diné as the Long Walk, brought thousands of Navajo from their Four Corners homeland across more than 300 miles of desert to a site that could not support them. We'll talk more about that history at the next stop — but as you drive through this corridor, it's worth knowing that these roads and this landscape were not just colonial trails. They were also, within living memory of people alive today, the routes of one of the largest forced relocations in American history.

Socorro sits at the northern edge of the Jornada del Muerto and the southern edge of the Rio Grande Bosque — the cottonwood forest that lines the river through central New Mexico. The ecological transition is visible if you're paying attention: the scrub and stone of the Jornada gives way, fairly abruptly, to the green corridor of the bosque. That green line has meant water and life to travelers for ten thousand years, and it still does.

sources.text: Socorro, New Mexico — Wikipedia

name: Navajo Long Walk & Bosque Redondo
body: The flat desert stretching east from the Rio Grande eventually becomes the Pecos River valley, about 150 miles to the east — and in the 1860s, a stretch of that valley was the site of one of the most catastrophic episodes in New Mexico history. It's worth knowing as you drive through this corridor, because the geography you're looking at was the geography that made it possible.

The Civil War in the East freed up a large Union military force in New Mexico by 1862, after Confederate forces were expelled following the Battle of Glorieta Pass near Santa Fe. General James Carleton, the new military commander of the Department of New Mexico, immediately turned that force toward what he called the 'Indian problem.' His plan was systematic: subdue each of the region's Indigenous nations in sequence. [1] The Mescalero Apache in the southeast went first. Then he turned to the Navajo.

The Diné — the Navajo — occupied a vast homeland centered in the Four Corners region, in what is now northwestern New Mexico and northeastern Arizona. Their territory, which they called Dinétah, is bounded by four sacred mountains that remain central to Navajo cosmology and identity. [2] They had maintained an uneasy relationship with Spanish and then Mexican colonial authorities for generations, and with American forces since 1846. By 1863, Carleton had had enough of the cycle.

He gave the Diné an ultimatum: relocate voluntarily to the Bosque Redondo reservation he was building on the Pecos River, or face war. Navajo leaders tried to explain that leaving Dinétah would sever their people's connection to the sacred geography that anchored their entire spiritual and cultural life. Carleton's response was to send Kit Carson into Navajo country with orders to destroy everything. [2]

Carson's campaign in the winter of 1863–1864 was a scorched-earth operation. His soldiers and Ute allies burned hogans and villages, slaughtered livestock, destroyed orchards and crops, and fouled water sources. They specifically targeted Canyon de Chelly — the great sandstone canyon system that was the heart of Navajo settlement — and stripped it bare. The Navajos called this period 'The Fearing Time.' [1] Facing starvation in freezing conditions, Navajo bands began surrendering by the thousands.

Beginning in the spring of 1864, the Army marched the surrendering Navajo to Bosque Redondo in 53 separate forced marches over three years. [1] The walks covered between 250 and 450 miles depending on the route. Soldiers pushed the columns at 12 to 15 miles per day. Those who fell behind — elders, the sick, women in labor — were shot rather than allowed to slow the column. An estimated 1,500 Diné died on the marches. By 1866, more than 9,000 people had been interned at Bosque Redondo. [1]

The reservation was a catastrophe from the start. The alkaline Pecos River water made people sick. The soil was too poor for the crops Carleton demanded. The timber within reach was stripped within months. The Navajo and the Mescalero Apache — traditional enemies — were confined together. Disease and starvation killed an estimated 2,000 people over four years. Carleton continued to insist the plan was working until he was relieved of command in 1867. [3]

In 1868, peace commissioners arrived at Bosque Redondo to negotiate. Navajo leader Barboncito made the case for return to their homeland in terms that moved even unsympathetic officials. The Treaty of Bosque Redondo was signed on June 1, 1868 — allowing the surviving Diné to return to a portion of their ancestral territory. [1] When the column of Navajo began the walk home, it stretched for ten miles. People wept when they saw the sacred peaks of Dinétah on the horizon. [4]

The Navajo Nation today is the largest Native American nation in the United States by land area — roughly 17 million acres across Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah — and has a population of around 400,000 enrolled members. [1] The Long Walk remains the central traumatic event in Diné historical memory, passed down through oral tradition in every generation. The Bosque Redondo Memorial at Fort Sumner, about 150 miles east of here, was designed by Navajo architect David Sloan in the shape of a hogan and a teepee, and is operated in partnership with the Diné and the Ndé — the Mescalero Apache — who were the first people imprisoned there. [3]

name: Very Large Array — Drive-By
body: About 50 miles to the west, on the Plains of San Agustín — a broad, flat, ancient lakebed surrounded by mountains — is one of the most recognizable scientific installations in the world: the Very Large Array, the Karl G. Jansky VLA. You can't see it from I-25, but knowing it's out there changes the quality of the landscape.

The VLA consists of 27 radio telescope dishes, each 82 feet in diameter, arranged in a Y-shaped configuration on railroad tracks that allow the dishes to be repositioned to different baseline lengths [1]. Together they function as a single synthetic aperture telescope. The array can span up to 22 miles across, giving it angular resolution comparable to the Hubble Space Telescope at optical wavelengths.

It has been observing since 1980, and the list of discoveries made with its data is long: the first detections of water ice on Mercury, the discovery of magnetic filaments near the galactic center, the first radio detection of a gamma-ray burst, and crucial observations used in building the first image of a black hole's shadow released in 2019 [2]. Carl Sagan used the VLA as the setting for the climactic scene of his novel Contact — the facility's staff have somewhat mixed feelings about the film version's depiction.

The VLA is open to the public for self-guided tours. If you want to add a detour on this trip, US-60 west from Socorro takes you to the site in about an hour. The scale of the dishes, standing in that flat expanse with the mountains all around, is genuinely impressive.

sources.text: Very Large Array — Wikipedia
VLA Is Turning 40 — National Science Foundation

name: San Antonio & Bosque del Apache
body: The little town you're passing is San Antonio, New Mexico — a different San Antonio from the Texas city, and considerably quieter. It's notable for two things: it's where Conrad Hilton was born on Christmas Day, 1887 [1], in a building his father ran as an early version of a hotel and general store. And it's the gateway to the Bosque del Apache National Wildlife Refuge, one of the great wildlife spectacles in North America.

The Bosque del Apache — the Apache forest — takes its name from the Apache camps that Spanish travelers documented along this stretch of the Rio Grande. The refuge was established in 1939 and covers about 57,000 acres of river bottom, wetlands, and desert upland along a stretch of the Rio Grande [2]. Its purpose is to restore and maintain the riparian habitat that once extended continuously along the river and has largely been drained, farmed, and channelized out of existence.

The refuge is famous for one thing above all others: the winter migration of sandhill cranes and snow geese. From November through February, tens of thousands of sandhill cranes and large flocks of snow geese use the Bosque del Apache as a wintering ground [2]. At dawn and dusk, the birds lift off the water in enormous flocks — the sound of thousands of sandhill crane calls carrying across the flat river bottom, the sky darkening with wings — in what wildlife photographers and birders describe as one of the most moving natural spectacles in the country.

The cranes have been using this valley as a stopover for far longer than the refuge has existed. Sandhill cranes appear in the rock art at sites throughout the Rio Grande corridor — the Pueblo people who lived here for centuries observed and depicted them with evident attention and care. In some Pueblo traditions, the crane is associated with water, longevity, and the passage between worlds.

The refuge also supports a remarkable diversity of other species year-round: roadrunners, bald and golden eagles in winter, mule deer, coyotes, and a recovering population of Rio Grande silvery minnow — an endangered fish that once ranged throughout the Rio Grande watershed and has been reduced to a small fraction of its original range by dams, diversions, and habitat loss.

The Festival of the Cranes is held here every November, drawing serious birders and casual visitors from across the country. If you're passing through in winter, even a short stop at one of the viewing areas along the auto tour route is worth it. The sound of the cranes alone is something that's hard to forget.

sources.text: Conrad Hilton — Wikipedia
Bosque del Apache National Wildlife Refuge — U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service

name: San Antonio — The Green Chile Burger
body: If you didn't stop at the Owl Bar in San Antonio — or if you did and are still thinking about it — here is the full story.

San Antonio, New Mexico, population roughly 160, sits at the northern edge of Bosque del Apache. It has a church, a grain elevator, and two restaurants that have become among the most argued-over food establishments in the Southwest. The Owl Bar & Café has been in operation since 1934. The Buckhorn Tavern opened across the street a few years later. Both serve green chile cheeseburgers. Both claim supremacy. The regulars on each side have strong opinions and are not shy about sharing them.

The Owl Bar's green chile burger became famous in the late 1940s, when workers from the White Sands Missile Range and the Trinity Site — 30 miles east across the Jornada del Muerto — would stop here after long shifts in the restricted desert. The connection is unusual: the most consequential scientific event of the 20th century took place 30 miles from here, and one of the things the workers did afterward was drive to San Antonio and eat a burger with roasted green chile on it. [1] The Buckhorn, for its part, has been declared the best burger in New Mexico by New Mexico Magazine, among others — though the Owl Bar's loyalists dispute the ranking. [2]

The basic formula at both places has not changed in 70 years: a beef patty, green chile roasted on-site, American cheese, a toasted bun. What makes it work is the specificity of the chile — roasted green chile has a smoky, vegetal heat that coats the meat in a way no sauce replicates. You can buy a better burger in most American cities. You cannot buy this one anywhere else.

sources.text: Owl Bar & Café, San Antonio, NM — New Mexico Magazine
Buckhorn Tavern: best burger in New Mexico — New Mexico Magazine

name: La Joya Wildlife Refuge
body: The wetlands and cottonwood bosque visible along the river to your left are part of the La Joya State Wildlife Management Area — a smaller, quieter counterpart to the Bosque del Apache, managed by the New Mexico Department of Game and Fish rather than the federal government [1].

La Joya — 'the jewel' — is about 4,000 acres of floodplain habitat along the Rio Grande, and it functions as part of the same migratory corridor that makes Bosque del Apache significant. Sandhill cranes, snow geese, ducks, and shorebirds use this stretch of the river too, though in smaller numbers. The lack of facilities or formal infrastructure means it sees a fraction of the visitors, which makes it a better choice if you want solitude.

The cottonwood trees lining the river here are Fremont cottonwoods — a species that has co-evolved with the boom-and-bust hydrology of desert rivers. Cottonwood seeds are viable for only a few days but are produced in enormous quantities and carried on the wind; a single tree can release millions of seeds in a season. The seeds need bare, moist soil to germinate — exactly what a flooding river provides when it recedes. The cottonwood bosque is, in a very direct sense, a product of the river's natural flood cycle, which is why river management and damming have profoundly affected the bosque's ability to regenerate.

sources.text: La Joya Wildlife Management Area — New Mexico Department of Game and Fish

name: Rio Grande Rift
body: For the past several hours, you've been traveling through one of the most significant geological features in North America — and it's largely invisible, which is part of why it doesn't get the attention it deserves. The Rio Grande Rift is a zone of active continental extension running roughly 600 miles from northern Mexico through New Mexico and into southern Colorado [1]. The Earth's crust here is being pulled apart.

A rift zone forms where tectonic forces stretch the crust thin enough that it begins to crack and subside. The rock on either side rises as the middle drops, creating the mountain-valley-mountain topography that has been your constant visual environment on this trip. Every basin you've driven through — the Tularosa, the Mesilla, the Jornada del Muerto, and the broader Rio Grande valley — is a graben, a block of crust that has dropped between parallel fault lines. The mountains flanking each basin are the uplifted edges, called horsts [1].

The Sandia Mountains above Albuquerque — which you'll see later today — are the most visually dramatic example. The west face of the Sandias is a fault scarp: the surface expression of the Sandia fault, which has displaced rock by as much as 25,000 vertical feet over the past several million years [1]. The Sandia granite exposed at the top of the range is about 1.4 billion years old. The sedimentary layers at the base of the range are roughly 300 million years old. The enormous gap between them represents millions of years of rock that have simply eroded away.

The rift is still active. Earthquake swarms are common in central New Mexico, and the hot springs at Truth or Consequences, Ojo Caliente, and several other locations along the rift are direct products of geothermal heat from the thinned, warm crust — water percolates down, heats up, and comes back up along fault pathways.

The Rio Grande itself occupies the rift valley. The river didn't create these basins; it simply found the lowest path through terrain that tectonics had already organized. In some stretches, the Rio Grande flows through an ancient lake bed — the rift basins filled with water periodically during wetter Pleistocene climates, and the flat, fertile soils of the Rio Grande valley are often the sediments of those ancient lakes.

New Mexico's geology is the key to understanding almost everything else about it — why people settled where they did, why the water is where it is, why the volcanoes are where they are, why the air is so clear. The rift gave this landscape its shape, and everything else followed from that.

sources.text: Rio Grande Rift — Wikipedia

name: The Peoples of the Río Grande — A Traveler's Map
body: You've been traveling through the territory of multiple distinct Indigenous nations for several days now. This corridor north of Socorro is a good moment to put the full picture together before you reach Albuquerque and the dense cluster of Pueblo communities to the north.

The Pueblo peoples of the Rio Grande are not a single nation — they're a collection of communities speaking at least five distinct language families. Tiwa is spoken at Taos, Picurís, Sandia, and Isleta, and — as you've seen — by the Tigua at Ysleta del Sur. Tewa is spoken at the six pueblos between Santa Fe and Taos: Ohkay Owingeh, San Ildefonso, Santa Clara, Nambé, Pojoaque, and Tesuque. Towa is spoken only at Jémez Pueblo, west of the Río Grande. And the Keres language is spoken at six pueblos in a wide arc around Albuquerque — Cochiti, Kewa, Zia, Santa Ana, San Felipe, and the more distant Acoma and Laguna. [1] These languages are mutually unintelligible. A Tewa speaker from Santa Clara cannot understand a Keres speaker from Kewa.

What these nations share is a pattern of life: settled, agricultural, organized around village governance and ceremonial life in kivas, with communities that have occupied the same sites — in some cases the same actual buildings — for hundreds of years. The earliest Rio Grande Pueblo sites date to roughly 750 CE; the major pueblos reached their current locations by around 1300 CE. [2]

To the south and east, the Apache nations represent a different cultural tradition entirely: semi-nomadic, organized around extended family bands, historically ranging across vast territories. You've already passed through Mescalero Apache country. The Jicarilla Apache hold land in the mountains to the northwest. To the west, the Diné — the Navajo — are the largest Native nation in the United States by land area, occupying a homeland of roughly 17 million acres in Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah. [3]

These nations have coexisted in this landscape for centuries — trading, sometimes fighting, always negotiating. When you stop at a pueblo, buy from a Native vendor, or walk through a trading post, you are engaging with communities that have maintained their cultural identity through everything this landscape has thrown at them. Pay attention to protocols around photography. Listen when guides explain what is and isn't permitted. The boundaries exist for reasons rooted in long experience.

sources.text: Pueblo peoples: languages and communities — Pueblo Cultural Center
Ancestral Pueblo chronology — Wikipedia
Navajo Nation: land area and population — Navajo Nation official

name: Albuquerque — Duke City
body: Welcome to Albuquerque — the largest city in New Mexico by a wide margin, home to about 560,000 people in the city proper and nearly a million in the metro area. It sits at about 5,300 feet elevation in the Rio Grande valley, with the Sandia Mountains rising immediately to the east and the West Mesa volcanoes defining the horizon to the west. The name comes from Francisco Fernández de la Cueva, the Duke of Alburquerque, a viceroy of New Spain — the extra 'r' dropped out over the centuries, which is why the city's nickname is the Duke City. [1]

The Spanish founded Albuquerque in 1706 as a villa on the Rio Grande. Old Town, which you can visit in the northwest part of the modern city, is where the original plaza was established. On the north side of that plaza stands the Church of San Felipe de Neri, completed in 1793 and in continuous use ever since — one of the oldest continually operating parishes in the United States. [2] The streets of Old Town have been walked continuously for over 300 years, by Spanish colonial families, Mexican citizens, American troops, and now tourists and art gallery browsers, all on essentially the same ground.

The railroad transformed Albuquerque entirely. When the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway arrived in 1880, it built its depot about two miles east of Old Town — and a new town grew up around it almost overnight. [2] That New Town incorporated in 1891 and eventually absorbed Old Town in 1920. The Spanish colonial plaza and the railroad freight depot ended up in the same city, separated by two miles and two centuries of architectural sensibility.

Route 66 came through Albuquerque in 1926, originally running north-south along 4th Street. In 1937 it was realigned to run directly east-west along Central Avenue, and Albuquerque suddenly had 18 miles of the Mother Road running through the heart of the city — the longest continuous urban stretch of Route 66 in the country. [3] The Nob Hill neighborhood, just east of downtown, has the best concentration of the classic Route 66 commercial vernacular — Streamline Moderne motels, vintage diners, neon Americana.

The city's modern economy rests heavily on military and federal installations. Kirtland Air Force Base was established in 1940, and Sandia National Laboratories followed in 1949 — technically a Department of Energy contractor, and one of the primary nuclear weapons research facilities in the country, continuing work that Los Alamos started in 1943. [1] The two installations are in many ways what built modern Albuquerque.

The Albuquerque International Balloon Fiesta, held every October, is the largest hot air balloon event in the world — roughly 800 balloons from dozens of countries, mass ascensions at dawn that fill the sky over the Rio Grande valley with color. The first gathering was a modest 13 balloons in a shopping center parking lot in 1972. [1] The event grew exponentially, driven in part by the famous Albuquerque Box — a meteorological pattern of wind currents at different altitudes over the Rio Grande valley that allows balloonists to fly north at low altitude and return south at high altitude, landing near their launch point.

The Sandia Mountains that rise directly east of the city are worth a word. The name means 'watermelon' — the granite turns a deep watermelon-pink at sunset, and the name is immediately obvious when you see it. The crest reaches 10,678 feet. The Sandia Peak Tramway, at 2.7 miles the longest aerial tramway in North America, runs from the northeast foothills to just below the crest in about 15 minutes. [4] The temperature difference between the bottom and the top is typically 25 to 30 degrees. On the crest in winter, there is a ski area. At the base, it is often T-shirt weather. Albuquerque may be the only major American city where you can ski and sunbathe on the same day without driving more than half an hour.

citations.text: ABQPedia (Albuquerque/Bernalillo County Public Library) — Sandia Peak Aerial Tramway
