name: Coronado's Winter Camp — Bernalillo & Kuaua Pueblo
body: Just off the highway in Bernalillo — a few miles north of Albuquerque — is the Coronado Historic Site, which marks one of the stranger episodes in the history of the Southwest: the place where Francisco Vásquez de Coronado wintered in 1540–41, pursuing a geographic fantasy built on a secondhand account told by an enslaved man.

Coronado left Mexico City in February 1540 with 340 Spanish soldiers, 1,300 Mexican Indian allies, and several thousand head of livestock, heading north to find the Seven Cities of Cíbola — the mythical cities of gold that a Franciscan friar named Marcos de Niza claimed to have seen from a distance. Cíbola, when Coronado reached it, turned out to be Háwikuh Pueblo in western New Mexico: a modest stone-and-adobe village whose residents pelted the approaching army with stones. [1] Coronado, furious and wounded, pressed on into the Rio Grande valley. He wintered at Kuaua Pueblo — a large Ancestral Pueblo settlement of around 1,200 rooms on the west bank of the Rio Grande here in Bernalillo.

The encounter was not peaceful. Coronado's forces demanded food and clothing from the surrounding Tiguex pueblos. When the Tiguex people resisted, Coronado's troops besieged the village, burned people at the stake, and conducted a campaign of violence that the Pueblo peoples of the Rio Grande would remember for 140 years — right up to the morning of August 10, 1680, when the Pueblo Revolt began. [2]

Kuaua Pueblo was abandoned before permanent Spanish colonization arrived, but the painted kiva murals recovered during archaeological excavations in the 1930s are originals — geometric, ceremonial, full of birds and rain imagery — and are among the finest examples of pre-contact Pueblo kiva art in existence. [3] They're displayed in the site museum, which is small, free, and genuinely worth the short detour off I-25.

sources.text: Francisco Vásquez de Coronado expedition — Wikipedia
Tiguex War, 1540–1541 — Wikipedia
Coronado Historic Site and Kuaua Pueblo murals — New Mexico Historic Sites

name: Kewa Pueblo (Santo Domingo)
body: You're approaching Kewa Pueblo — known for most of American history as Santo Domingo Pueblo, and now increasingly using its Keres name. Kewa is one of the largest of the Rio Grande Pueblos, with an enrolled population of around 4,500 people, and it occupies a position in the middle of the historic trade network that connected the different Pueblo nations [1].

Kewa has long been known as one of the great jewelry-making communities of the Southwest. The heishi bead — tiny, thin, hand-ground discs of shell, turquoise, jet, or coral strung on a single thread — was developed here and is still made by Kewa artisans using techniques passed down over generations [1]. The Kewa also produce distinctive silver and turquoise jewelry and are major participants in the Indian Market in Santa Fe, the largest juried Native American art market in the world.

The pueblo has a complicated relationship with tourism and outside attention. The village itself is not open to casual visitors; there's no public access to the residential areas or the kiva structures at the center of the community. The feast day of Santo Domingo in August is a major public event that draws thousands of people, but the pueblo maintains careful control over what is and isn't photographed, recorded, or described in public. They've had 400 years of experience with outsiders who wanted to document, appropriate, or sell their culture, and they've developed protocols accordingly.

The road between Albuquerque and Santa Fe passes through Kewa territory — the corridor of cottonwood and mesa that lines I-25 through this stretch has been Kewa land since long before the highway existed. The proximity to the main transportation artery has made the pueblo a significant player in New Mexico's roadside economy: the Kewa Trading Post at the highway exit is a legitimate first stop for turquoise and heishi jewelry.

sources.text: Kewa Pueblo (Santo Domingo) — Wikipedia

name: Kasha-Katuwe Tent Rocks
body: Turn here for Tent Rocks — or if you've already taken the detour, welcome back to the highway. Either way, here is what you should know about the place.

Kasha-Katuwe Tent Rocks National Monument sits on land managed in partnership with Kewa Pueblo, and the name is Keres for 'white cliffs.' The tent rocks themselves are cone-shaped pinnacles of volcanic tuff — compacted ash and pumice ejected from a series of volcanic eruptions in the Jemez Mountains six to seven million years ago [1]. Each cone is capped by a harder rock fragment that protects the softer tuff below from erosion. Remove the cap and the cone erodes away. Keep the cap and the cone can stand for thousands of years, growing taller relative to the surrounding material as erosion strips away everything without a protector.

The result is an eerie, intimate landscape: narrow slot canyons cutting through pale volcanic rock, opening periodically into amphitheaters filled with dozens of these cones, ranging from a few feet tall to nearly 90 feet. The rock is soft enough to mark with your fingernail, which makes the sheer height of the formations feel improbable.

The trail through Tent Rocks is one of the better short hikes in New Mexico — about 3 miles round trip, with a steady climb through the slot canyon section and a rewarding viewpoint at the top overlooking the Jemez Mountains and the Rio Grande valley [1]. It's strenuous enough to feel like an accomplishment but accessible enough that the trailhead is genuinely crowded on weekends.

The Jemez volcanic field — the source of all this ash — is one of the largest calderas in North America, about 13 miles across [2]. Valles Caldera, visible from parts of the Jemez Mountains to the northwest, is the collapsed remnant of the volcanic chamber that produced the eruptions. The Jemez system is still geothermally active.
The monument has restricted hours and requires a timed entry permit on weekends from spring through fall — check the BLM website before you go.

sources.text: Kasha-Katuwe Tent Rocks National Monument — BLM
Valles Caldera — Wikipedia

name: La Cienega & the Santa Fe River
body: You're approaching Santa Fe along the route of the old Camino Real, and the landscape you're driving through — low piñon and juniper hills, red earth, the distant outline of the Jemez Mountains to the northwest — is the landscape that Spanish colonists found when they established their capital here in 1610.

The Santa Fe River, which flows through the city, is the reason the capital was put here rather than somewhere else. It's a modest stream — barely a creek in dry years — but it provided reliable water at the base of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, with access to timber on the slopes above and agricultural land in the valley below. The acequia system — the network of irrigation ditches that Spanish colonists and their Pueblo neighbors built and maintained for centuries — still functions in communities along the Santa Fe River and throughout northern New Mexico. Some of the acequia organizations operating today trace their governance structures directly to Spanish colonial water law.

The road you're on into Santa Fe broadly follows the route of the Santa Fe Trail — the commercial highway that connected Missouri to New Mexico from 1821 until the railroad arrived in 1880. Where El Camino Real ran north-south between Mexico City and Santa Fe, the Santa Fe Trail ran east-west between Missouri and Santa Fe, and together they made Santa Fe the intersection of two of the great overland trade routes of 19th-century North America. [1] The Trail opened in 1821, the same year Mexico achieved independence from Spain and lifted the trade restrictions that had made New Mexico effectively inaccessible to American commerce. Within two years, wagon trains of American manufactured goods were rolling across the Kansas plains toward Santa Fe, where they traded for silver, furs, mules, and Navajo blankets.

The road you're on into Santa Fe broadly follows the route of the Santa Fe Trail — the commercial highway that connected Missouri to New Mexico from 1821 until the railroad arrived in 1880. Where El Camino Real ran north-south between Mexico City and Santa Fe, the Santa Fe Trail ran east-west between Missouri and Santa Fe, and together they made Santa Fe the intersection of two of the great overland trade routes of 19th-century North America. [3] The Trail opened in 1821, the same year Mexico achieved independence from Spain and lifted the trade restrictions that had made New Mexico effectively inaccessible to American commerce. Within two years, wagon trains of American manufactured goods were rolling across the Kansas plains toward Santa Fe, where they traded for silver, furs, mules, and Navajo blankets.

In August 1846, General Stephen Kearny marched the Army of the West down the Santa Fe Trail with about 1,600 soldiers and took New Mexico from Mexico without firing a shot. The Mexican governor, Manuel Armijo, evacuated Santa Fe rather than fight. Kearny rode into the plaza, raised the American flag over the Palace of the Governors, and declared New Mexico a U.S. territory. [3] The whole operation took six weeks from Fort Leavenworth, Kansas to Santa Fe — accomplished, essentially, on a road that American merchants had been traveling for 25 years.
CITATION_BLOCK_27

In August 1846, General Stephen Kearny marched the Army of the West down the Santa Fe Trail with about 1,600 soldiers and took New Mexico from Mexico without firing a shot. The Mexican governor, Manuel Armijo, evacuated Santa Fe rather than fight. Kearny rode into the plaza, raised the American flag over the Palace of the Governors, and declared New Mexico a U.S. territory. [1] The whole operation took six weeks from Fort Leavenworth, Kansas to Santa Fe — accomplished, essentially, on a road that American merchants had been traveling for 25 years.

La Cienega — 'the marsh' — is the community just southwest of Santa Fe where the Santa Fe River emerges from the mountains with enough flow to support farming. El Rancho de las Golondrinas, which you'll pass on the return leg, sits on the acequia here and was a historic stopping point — a paraje — on El Camino Real, the last major way station before reaching the capital.

Santa Fe has been the capital of New Mexico continuously since 1610, making it the oldest continuously occupied state capital in the United States — 10 years older than Plymouth Colony, 96 years older than American independence. The Palace of the Governors on the central plaza was built in 1610 and is the oldest public building in continuous use in the country. You're entering a city that is genuinely old by American standards, which is easy to miss because it doesn't look like the old cities of the American East.

sources.text: Santa Fe Trail Association — History of the Trail
Santa Fe Trail Association — History

name: Santa Fe — Canyon Road, Indian Market & the Art Capital
body: Santa Fe is small — about 85,000 people — and it runs on art, tourism, state government, and a quality of afternoon light that painters have been chasing for a century. It is also, depending on how you measure, between 400 and 500 years older than most American cities.

The Palace of the Governors on the central plaza was built in 1610 and is the oldest public building in continuous use in the United States. [1] The long portal across the front has been used by Native vendors since at least the 19th century, and today is managed as a juried program — artisans from the pueblo and tribal nations of New Mexico hold assigned spaces and sell work made by their own hands. If you're going to buy turquoise or silver anywhere on this trip, buy it here. You are buying directly from the maker, under a program with real standards.

Canyon Road, a 10-minute walk from the plaza, is a mile-long street of galleries, studios, and restaurants that has been the center of Santa Fe's art market since the 1960s. [2] By most counts, Santa Fe has the third-largest art market in the United States after New York and Los Angeles. The galleries range from internationally connected contemporary operations to tourist-grade souvenir shops — the full range is visible on a single walk up the street.

The Indian Market, held every August on the plaza weekend and run by the Southwestern Association for Indian Arts since 1922, is the largest juried Native American art market in the world — around 1,100 artists, up to 150,000 visitors over a single weekend. [3] The work ranges from traditional pottery, weaving, and silverwork to contemporary fine art. The market has a competitive, serious dimension that first-time visitors often don't expect.

Santa Fe's built environment is subject to a design code requiring Pueblo Revival or Territorial style for most new construction — the result of a 1957 ordinance. [4] The adobe walls, the exposed vigas, the earth tones are not an accident. They are a municipal policy, and one that has kept the city looking coherent if occasionally self-conscious.

name: Pojoaque & Nambé Pueblos
body: The small communities along US-84/285 north of Santa Fe include two of the Eight Northern Pueblos — Pojoaque and Nambé — and their modern presence along a busy highway corridor is itself a form of historical statement.

Pojoaque Pueblo was effectively destroyed in the late 17th and early 18th centuries by smallpox epidemics, drought, and the disruptions of the Spanish colonial period. [1] By the early 20th century, the population had dropped to a handful of families, and federal authorities twice attempted to terminate the tribal government and absorb the land into surrounding communities. The Pueblo survived through the determination of those remaining families to maintain their legal identity and land base, and the population has rebounded to several thousand enrolled members. [2] The Cities of Gold Casino and resort complex on the highway is the primary economic engine of the modern pueblo.

Nambé Pueblo sits a few miles to the east, up the drainage of the Nambé River, and is one of the least-visited of the northern pueblos despite its proximity to Santa Fe. The Nambé people are Tewa speakers — part of the same linguistic group as Pojoaque, Tesuque, San Ildefonso, Ohkay Owingeh, and Santa Clara. [3] The Tewa pueblos form a coherent cultural and linguistic cluster across the Rio Grande basin north of Santa Fe.

The Nambé Falls, above the pueblo, are among the more spectacular waterfall features in northern New Mexico — a series of three falls dropping through red rock canyons. [4] They're accessible by permit through the pueblo, and the fee goes directly to tribal programs. It's the kind of tourism arrangement that the northern pueblos have gotten increasingly good at managing on their own terms — access in exchange for revenue, on tribal schedules, with tribal guides available but not mandatory.

sources.text: Pojoaque Pueblo history — Eight Northern Indian Pueblos Council

name: Los Alamos & the Manhattan Project
body: Up on the mesa to your west — that flat-topped plateau above the Pajarito canyon system — is Los Alamos. And if you're trying to understand the 20th century, this particular mesa is one of the places where you have to eventually arrive.

But before Los Alamos, this mesa and the land below it had a different kind of history. The colonization of New Mexico began practically in this corridor. In 1598, Don Juan de Oñate led the founding expedition of New Mexico north from Mexico with 500 soldiers, colonists, and Franciscan priests, plus 7,000 head of livestock. He established the first colonial capital at Ohkay Owingeh — the pueblo you saw or will see on the return leg — took the pueblo's buildings for his own use, and began the forced labor system that would define Spanish colonial New Mexico for the next 80 years. [4] Oñate's legacy is one of the most contested in the American Southwest. A large bronze equestrian statue of him stood in Alcalde, a few miles north of here, for decades. In December 1997, an activist group calling themselves Friends of Acoma sawed off the statue's right foot with an electric saw and left a note reading 'Fair is fair' — a direct reference to Oñate's order that the men of Acoma Pueblo have their right feet amputated after the 1599 battle. [5] The statue was eventually restored, removed entirely in 2020 amid national protests, and re-erected in Española in 2023. The debate about what New Mexico should do with Oñate continues.
CITATION_BLOCK_29

Before the Manhattan Project, Los Alamos was a boys' ranch school — the Los Alamos Ranch School, founded in 1917, which educated the sons of wealthy Eastern families in an outdoor curriculum that emphasized horses, manual labor, and the restorative qualities of high desert air. [1] J. Robert Oppenheimer had hiked this country as a young man and knew the mesa. When the Army needed an isolated, defensible site for a secret weapons laboratory in 1942, he suggested it. The school was given 30 days notice to vacate.

In the spring of 1943, Oppenheimer arrived as scientific director of what the Army called Project Y — the laboratory that would design and build an atomic bomb. [2] Within weeks, the mesa was swarming with physicists: Hans Bethe, Richard Feynman, Enrico Fermi, Niels Bohr, Edward Teller, and dozens of others, many of them refugees from Nazi Europe who had particular motivation to ensure that the United States got there before Germany. The entire community was classified; residents mailed letters to a P.O. box in Santa Fe; the mesa didn't officially exist.

The technical challenge they faced was staggering. Uranium-235 and plutonium-239 undergo fission when struck by neutrons, releasing energy and more neutrons in a chain reaction. The question was whether you could assemble a sufficient mass of fissile material fast enough — before the chain reaction blew the assembly apart — to produce a nuclear explosion rather than just a radioactive disaster. Two different design approaches were pursued simultaneously: a gun-type design for the uranium bomb and an implosion design for the plutonium bomb. The implosion design was more efficient but vastly more complex — it required achieving a perfectly symmetrical compression of the plutonium core from all directions simultaneously, using precisely shaped explosive lenses.

The gun-type bomb — Little Boy — was considered so reliable that it was never tested before use. The implosion bomb — Fat Man — was tested at Trinity Site on July 16, 1945. [3] Trinity was the first nuclear detonation in history. Three weeks later, Little Boy was dropped on Hiroshima on August 6, killing approximately 70,000 people immediately. [4] Fat Man was dropped on Nagasaki on August 9, killing approximately 40,000 more. [5] Japan surrendered on August 15.

The moral weight of those decisions has never fully settled. Oppenheimer himself became a deeply conflicted figure after the war, publicly questioning the development of the hydrogen bomb and eventually having his security clearance revoked during the McCarthy-era hearings of 1954 — a verdict that was formally rescinded by the Biden administration in 2022. [6] The scientists who built the bomb generally believed they were ending a war that might otherwise have required an invasion of Japan killing hundreds of thousands on both sides. Critics have argued since the 1940s that Japan was already defeated and that the bombings were as much a demonstration to the Soviet Union as a military necessity.

Los Alamos National Laboratory still exists and is still primarily a nuclear weapons laboratory, though its mission now includes stockpile stewardship — maintaining the safety and reliability of the existing arsenal without testing — as well as research in energy, biology, and materials science. [7] The town that grew around the laboratory is a federal company town of about 13,000 people, with unusually high rates of advanced degrees and some of the most expensive real estate in New Mexico.

The Bradbury Science Museum in Los Alamos is free, well-done, and covers both the Manhattan Project and the laboratory's current work. [8] If you want more depth on the history, the Fuller Lodge — the original log building that served as the dining hall for the Ranch School and then the social center of the wartime laboratory — is preserved and open. It's one of the few physical links to the place as it actually was in 1943.

name: Bandelier National Monument
body: Bandelier National Monument sits in the canyon system below the Los Alamos mesa, and the proximity of the two is one of the more jarring juxtapositions New Mexico offers: a place where ancestral Pueblo people built their homes in cliffside caves 700 years ago, directly below a mesa where the 20th century built a weapon capable of destroying a city.

The name comes from Adolph Bandelier, a Swiss-American archaeologist who explored this canyon in the 1880s and wrote a novel — The Delight Makers — that was an early attempt to imagine Pueblo life in the prehistoric period. [1] The monument preserves the ruins of Tyuonyi, a circular pueblo that once rose two or three stories in the floor of Frijoles Canyon, and the remarkable cliff dwellings carved directly into the soft volcanic tuff of the canyon walls — rooms, alcoves, and cave homes dug and pecked out of the rock by hand.

The people who lived here were among the ancestors of the modern Pueblo peoples — specifically, traditions maintained by Cochiti, Kewa, San Ildefonso, Santa Clara, and other Tewa and Keres pueblos trace portions of their ancestry to the communities of the Pajarito Plateau. The cliffside rooms were occupied roughly from 1150 to 1550 CE, after which the population consolidated into the larger pueblos along the Rio Grande. [2] The reasons for this shift are still debated — drought, soil exhaustion, social reorganization — and probably involved all of these.

The ladders you climb to access the cave dwellings are modern, but the hand-and-toe-hold trails pecked into the cliff face beside them are original — worn smooth by centuries of use. The smallest caves are the size of a closet; the largest, called the Ceremonial Cave, sits 140 feet above the canyon floor and contains a reconstructed kiva. [3]

The monument suffered severe damage in the Las Conchas wildfire of 2011, which burned roughly 150,000 acres in the Jemez Mountains and sent walls of debris down Frijoles Canyon, damaging the visitor center and infrastructure. [4] The recovery took years. The landscape in the upper reaches of the monument still shows the characteristic post-fire look — standing dead trees, recovering understory — that is increasingly common throughout the forests of the American Southwest.
Bandelier requires a timed entry permit from May through October, and shuttle buses run from the White Rock Visitor Center on NM-4. [5] It's about 45 minutes to an hour from Santa Fe.

name: Chimayó — Lourdes of America
body: You're approaching Chimayó, and there's almost nowhere in New Mexico where the layers of history sit quite so visibly on top of one another as they do here.

Start with the land itself. The Tewa-speaking Pueblo people who lived in this valley long before the Spanish arrived considered this ground sacred. Specifically, they believed that a particular spot in the valley contained a pool of healing mud — poshu owingeh, a place of warm mud bubbling up from the earth — with curative powers. [1]

When the Spanish settled the Río Grande valley in the 17th and 18th centuries, they brought Catholicism with them — and in the way that often happened on this frontier, the new faith and the old one didn't so much replace each other as negotiate a shared geography. The Santuario de Chimayó, which you're passing, was built between 1814 and 1816 on or near the site the Tewa had considered sacred. The story that crystallized around it — that a friar found a crucifix buried in a hole in the ground here, and that the hole refilled with holy earth whenever dirt was removed — is almost certainly a Christianized version of the older Pueblo tradition. [2]

Inside the Santuario, there's a small room called the pocito — the little well. In the floor there is a hole, and pilgrims reach down into that hole and take a handful of the sacred dirt. They rub it on their bodies, bring it home for sick relatives, tuck it into envelopes and mail it across the country. The room surrounding the pocito is hung with crutches, braces, and photographs — ex-votos left by people who believe they were healed here.

The Santuario draws an estimated 300,000 visitors a year, making it the most visited Catholic site in the United States. [3] During Holy Week — the week before Easter — as many as 30,000 pilgrims walk here on foot from Santa Fe, Albuquerque, and communities across New Mexico and southern Colorado. Some carry crosses. Some walk barefoot. The pilgrimage has been continuous, in various forms, since the Santuario was built.

Chimayó has another tradition that has nothing to do with the church: weaving. The Ortega and Trujillo families — two of the great weaving dynasties of the Southwest — have been producing textiles in this valley for eight or nine generations. Chimayó weaving developed its distinctive palette and pattern language from a combination of Spanish colonial traditions and the Pueblo weaving that predated them. [4] You may have passed one of the weaving shops on the road in — Ortega's or Trujillo's, both of which still operate out of family compounds and sell work made on the premises.

Chimayó is small, quiet, and easy to drive past without fully registering what you're in the middle of. But this valley has been a place of pilgrimage — first Tewa, then Catholic, sometimes both simultaneously — for longer than the United States has existed. That continuity is worth sitting with for a moment.

El Santuario de Chimayó: history and construction — Wikipedia

name: Truchas & the High Road Villages
body: You're in the heart of the High Road to Taos now — and the villages you're passing through are among the oldest continuously occupied Hispanic communities in the United States.

The High Road follows a ridge of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains through a series of villages that were settled in the 17th and 18th centuries by Spanish colonial families given land grants by the provincial government in Santa Fe. These grants established communities at Chimayó, Truchas, Las Trampas, Chamisal, Ojo Sarco, and a dozen other places in the mountains — each one an isolated agricultural settlement clustered around a church and an acequia, surrounded by communal grazing land called ejidos. [1]

Truchas — which means 'trout,' for the streams that run down from the mountains — sits at nearly 8,000 feet elevation on a ridge with views that extend west to the Jemez Mountains and east to the high peaks of the Sangre de Cristos. Robert Redford filmed The Milagro Beanfield War here in 1987, and the village's appearance in that film has given it a certain romanticized quality in the regional imagination. [2] It is genuinely beautiful, with the old adobe buildings along the main road and the mountain backdrop, but it is also a living community with the complicated economic reality of a rural New Mexico mountain village.

The broader region these villages occupy is sometimes called the Hispano homeland — the core of the territory settled by Spanish colonial families and their descendants, who maintained a distinct cultural identity through the Mexican period and the American period and continue to do so. The land grant history of northern New Mexico is a long story of legal dispossession: the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo in 1848 guaranteed the property rights of Mexican citizens in the ceded territory, but in practice, the ejido lands — the communal lands that were central to the village economy — were systematically transferred to the federal government and private Anglo-American owners over the following decades, through a combination of legal manipulation, tax sales, and outright fraud. [3]

The land grant movement of the 1960s, led by Reies López Tijerina, sought to reclaim those ejido lands through direct action — including a famous courthouse raid in Tierra Amarilla in 1967. [4] The legal claims have never been fully resolved, and the relationship between these mountain communities and the federal lands that surround them remains complicated. The Forest Service manages land that many families in these villages believe was taken from their ancestors illegally.

Reies López Tijerina and the Tierra Amarilla courthouse raid, 1967 — Wikipedia

name: Las Trampas
body: Pull over in Las Trampas if you can — it takes five minutes, and the church is worth it.

The Iglesia de San José de Gracia in Las Trampas was built between 1760 and 1776 by twelve founding families who established the village under a land grant from the Governor of New Mexico. [1] The church they built is considered the finest surviving example of Spanish colonial church architecture in the United States — the original structure, almost entirely intact, with its original twin bell towers, its massive adobe walls up to five feet thick, its carved wooden interior, and the hand-painted altar screen called a reredos that covers the wall behind the main altar.

The church has survived because the community has maintained it continuously. No major restorations, no interventions by outside preservationists — just a village that kept its church in repair for two and a half centuries. The acequia that irrigates the fields around Las Trampas is the same acequia the founding families dug in the 1750s. The land belongs to descendants of the original grantees. [2]

The interior of San José de Gracia is painted in earth colors — the same ochres, reds, and cream whites available from the local soils — and the woodwork is carved in the unpretentious, competent manner of frontier craftsmen working from memory and tradition rather than architectural drawings. There are no professional flourishes. The proportions are slightly off in ways that feel human rather than designed. It is the kind of building that has more presence than its simple description suggests.

Las Trampas has fewer than 100 residents now. The church is still the center of the community, still used for Mass, still maintained by the families who have done so for eight generations. The acequia still runs in the spring and summer. Some things in northern New Mexico are genuinely continuous.

sources.text: San José de Gracia Church, Las Trampas — New Mexico Historic Preservation Division
Las Trampas historic district — Wikipedia

name: Ranchos de Taos — San Francisco de Asís
body: You're coming into Ranchos de Taos, the agricultural settlement a few miles south of Taos proper, and the church on your left — San Francisco de Asís — is one of the most photographed buildings in North America.

The adobe buttresses on the back of the church are the part you've probably seen in photographs. They're massive, rounded, almost organic — they look less like architectural details than like the earth itself rising up to support the walls. Ansel Adams photographed them. Georgia O'Keeffe painted them multiple times. Paul Strand photographed them in 1932 in an image that's in the Museum of Modern Art. [1] The church has appeared in more art books, photography collections, and coffee table publications than almost any other building in the American West.

It was built in the early 19th century — most historians date the main construction between 1815 and 1826, though there was an earlier chapel on the site. [2] The building material is adobe: sun-dried mud bricks plastered with mud stucco that must be re-coated every few years to maintain the walls against New Mexico's summer thunderstorms. The community of Ranchos de Taos has been recoating those walls since the building was new — an annual collective effort called an enjarre that is as much a community ritual as a maintenance task.

The church is still active, still serving the parish of Ranchos de Taos. Mass is said here regularly. The interior has a painted retablo behind the main altar and a painting called Shadow of the Cross — a 19th-century work in which a faint cruciform shape appears on the figure of Christ under ultraviolet or low light — that has been a popular subject for mystical interpretation. [3]
Stop if you can. Walk around the back and look at those buttresses from the east. The photographers weren't wrong.

sources.text: San Francisco de Asís Mission Church: history and iconography — Wikipedia
San Francisco de Asís Church — NPS National Register of Historic Places
Shadow of the Cross painting, Ranchos de Taos — Taos Visitor Center

name: Taos Pueblo
body: You're approaching Taos Pueblo — and if this is the only thing you stop for in Taos, that is the right choice.

The two great house complexes of Taos Pueblo — Hlauuma to the north of the creek and Hlaukwima to the south — have been lived in for somewhere between 1,000 and 1,500 years, with archaeological evidence pointing to continuous occupation of the current site since at least 1000 CE. [1] The buildings rise four and five stories in terraced tiers of the same tan adobe as the surrounding high desert. At dusk, with the Sangre de Cristo Mountains going pink behind them, the two house blocks look less like something built than something that grew from the ground.

The Taos people are northern Tiwa speakers — linguistic relatives of the Tigua at Ysleta you visited in El Paso, though separated by geography and centuries of separate history. About 150 residents still live in the old pueblo without electricity or running water in their rooms, by preference. [2] The communal water supply comes from Red Willow Creek, the stream that runs through the middle of the compound, which originates in the mountains above and is itself sacred.

The most important chapter in Taos Pueblo's modern history is the return of Blue Lake. Mä-wha-luu — Blue Lake — is a glacial lake high in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains above the pueblo, the source of Red Willow Creek, and the center of Taos ceremonial life for centuries. In 1906 the U.S. government absorbed the land into the Carson National Forest, and the Taos people were given access as mere visitors to their own sacred ground. [3]

The pueblo spent 64 years fighting for its return. They refused cash settlements, including an offer of $10 million, on the grounds that no amount of money was equivalent to a sacred place. In 1970, Congress passed the Taos Pueblo Blue Lake Bill, returning 48,000 acres including Blue Lake to the pueblo — the first return of land to a Native nation for spiritual reasons in American history. President Nixon signed it as a priority of his Indian policy. [3]

The path from the visitor center to the old pueblo is a five-minute walk. Photography is permitted in common areas but not inside buildings or kivas. The $25 admission goes directly to the pueblo. [4] Take your time.

sources.text: Taos Pueblo: history and archaeology — Wikipedia
Taos Pueblo UNESCO World Heritage Site — UNESCO
Blue Lake and the Taos Pueblo Blue Lake Act, 1970 — Wikipedia
Taos Pueblo visitor information — Taos Pueblo official

name: Rio Grande Gorge Bridge
body: The bridge you're crossing — or approaching — is the Rio Grande Gorge Bridge, and the view straight down from the railing is 565 feet to the river below. When it was completed in 1965, it was the second-highest bridge in the United States. [1] It's now somewhere around the seventh or eighth, depending on how you measure, but it remains one of the most vertiginous roadway spans in the country.

The gorge it crosses is a product of the Rio Grande Rift — the same continental extension zone that created the mountain-basin topography of southern New Mexico. Here in the Taos Plateau region, the Rio Grande has cut down through several hundred feet of basalt lava flows that poured out of the Jemez volcanic field roughly three to four million years ago. [2] The result is a slot canyon of dark, columnar basalt with the river running through it — narrow, fast, and almost completely hidden from the surrounding plateau until you're standing at the edge.

The Taos Plateau itself is largely flat — a high grassland at about 7,000 feet elevation, with the Sangre de Cristo Mountains visible to the east and the volcanic landscape of the Jemez to the southwest. Driving west from Taos, you cross miles of open plateau with no visible indication that there's a 565-foot chasm ahead of you. Then suddenly the bridge is there and the ground disappears.

The Taos Pueblo and other communities in the region have used the gorge for centuries as a travel barrier — crossing points were few, controlled, and strategic. The modern bridge is only one of two road crossings in the entire stretch of gorge between Taos and the Colorado border. [3]

The Rio Grande Gorge is a National Wild and Scenic River corridor for much of this stretch, and the Class III and IV whitewater below the bridge draws rafters and kayakers from mid-spring through early summer when snowmelt peaks. [4] The rim trail along the east edge of the gorge is one of the better short walks near Taos.

name: Ojo Caliente Mineral Springs
body: You're approaching Ojo Caliente — 'hot eye' in Spanish, a name for the spring that bubbles up from the earth at the edge of this small village. The springs here have been in continuous use for at least 2,000 years; a large ancestral Pueblo site called Posi-ouinge once occupied the mesa above the springs, and the spring itself was considered sacred. [1]

What makes Ojo Caliente unusual among New Mexico's hot springs is the variety of mineral content in different springs on the same small site. The springs here include lithia, iron, arsenic, sodium, and soda sources — each with different temperature and mineral composition. [2] The Tiwa people and later the Spanish attributed different therapeutic properties to each. The arsenic spring, in particular, was prescribed for a range of conditions in the 19th century — arsenic in small doses was a recognized remedy in period medicine, however horrifying that sounds now.

The Ojo Caliente Mineral Springs Resort is the current manifestation of what has been, in various forms, a healing destination since the Spanish colonial period. The resort is relatively modest — this is not a luxury spa in the global sense, but a working mineral springs operation with outdoor pools, private soaking tubs, and a hotel that has grown incrementally over decades. The clientele is a mix of northern New Mexico locals, Santa Fe day-trippers, and travelers who specifically seek out the more meditative, less polished end of the wellness spectrum.

The setting is extraordinary — red sandstone cliffs, cottonwood bosque, the Chama River drainage visible in the middle distance. Georgia O'Keeffe stayed here, as she stayed at most of the significant landscape sites between Abiquiu and Taos. The light in the late afternoon turns the cliffs the color of embers.

sources.text: Posi-ouinge ancestral Pueblo site near Ojo Caliente — NM State Historic Preservation Office
Ojo Caliente Mineral Springs: geology and history — Wikipedia

name: Ghost Ranch & Georgia O'Keeffe Country
body: The landscape you're driving through now is the landscape that Georgia O'Keeffe spent 40 years trying to paint. This particular stretch of the Piedra Lumbre valley — the 'stone of light' — between Abiquiu and Ghost Ranch is where the Chama River has cut through a sequence of Triassic and Jurassic sedimentary rocks in colors that don't look real. Red, orange, cream, purple, khaki, chalk white — the layers stack in horizontal bands, interbedded with volcanic intrusions and eroded into cliffs, mesas, and badlands that change appearance every hour as the light shifts.

O'Keeffe first visited New Mexico in 1917 with her friend Leah Harris, but the visit that changed everything was in 1929, when she came at the invitation of Mabel Dodge Luhan, the wealthy socialite and patron who had established a kind of creative salon in Taos. [1] O'Keeffe spent that summer in Taos, then returned for several more summers, painting bones and flowers and the landscapes around her. In 1934 she discovered Ghost Ranch, 14 miles north of Abiquiu, and in 1940 she bought a small house on the property. She spent every summer there for the rest of her life.

Ghost Ranch takes its name from the brujos — witches — that local legend held responsible for the deaths of livestock on the property in the early 20th century. The name stuck through the various ownerships and eventually became a cattle ranch operated by a New York dude ranch operator named Arthur Pack. In 1955, Pack donated the property to the Presbyterian Church, which operates it today as a conference and retreat center. [2] You can stay there, take workshops, and hike the trails that O'Keeffe hiked — including the trail to Chimney Rock and the trail out to the red and yellow cliffs she called the Piedernal country.

The landscape here became so central to O'Keeffe's work that it's now almost impossible to look at these cliffs without thinking of her paintings. The mesa she called the Black Place — a long drive west into the Navajo country — was the subject of some of her most severe and abstract work. Pedernal, the flat-topped volcanic mountain visible to the south from Ghost Ranch, appears in her paintings repeatedly. She reportedly said 'God told me if I painted it enough, I could have it.' When she died in 1986 at age 98, her ashes were scattered on its summit. [3]

The geology here is extraordinary in its own right. Ghost Ranch sits atop one of the most significant dinosaur fossil deposits in North America — the Chinle Formation, a series of Late Triassic mudstones and siltstones that were once the flood plains of Pangea. The Ghost Ranch quarry has produced thousands of specimens of Coelophysis bauri, a small, fast bipedal predator that lived about 210 million years ago and is one of the earliest known dinosaurs. New Mexico designated Coelophysis as its state fossil in 1981. [4] The Ruth Hall Museum of Paleontology at Ghost Ranch has specimens and context if you want to go down that path.

O'Keeffe's studio house in Abiquiu — about 12 miles south of Ghost Ranch — is maintained by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe and offers tours by reservation. The garden wall, the patio door, the view from the upstairs window that she painted over and over — these are all there, basically unchanged. The tour is worth booking in advance if you can. [5]

Ghost Ranch history — Ghost Ranch official
Georgia O'Keeffe: life and legacy — Wikipedia
Coelophysis bauri and Ghost Ranch quarry — New Mexico Museum of Natural History
O'Keeffe Abiquiu Home and Studio tours — Georgia O'Keeffe Museum

name: Abiquiu Lake & Plaza Blanca
body: Abiquiu Lake, just ahead on your right, is a reservoir created by Abiquiu Dam on the Chama River — completed by the Army Corps of Engineers in 1963, primarily for flood control and water storage. [1] Unlike the older Rio Grande reservoirs at Elephant Butte and Caballo, Abiquiu is in the mountains rather than the desert, and the surrounding landscape gives it a different quality. The red Entrada sandstone cliffs above the waterline are the same formation that O'Keeffe painted throughout her career here.

The village of Abiquiu sits on the mesa above the reservoir — an old Spanish land-grant community with a history reaching back to the 18th century. It was also, improbably, the site of a significant Genízaro settlement. The Genízaros were detribalized Indigenous people — taken from their communities through capture, ransom, or purchase, often as children — who were absorbed into Spanish colonial society as servants and laborers and eventually formed their own communities on the margins of the colonial settlements. Abiquiu was one of the few places where Genízaro families were given formal land grants, and the community's mixed Indigenous and Spanish heritage is still present in its cultural practices. [2]

About a mile east of the highway, accessible on a dirt road, is Plaza Blanca — 'the white place.' This is a formation of white Abiquiu Formation tuff and volcanic ash eroded into spires, fins, and canyon walls of chalky white rock. O'Keeffe called it her 'white place' and returned to it repeatedly. The Presbyterian Ghost Ranch organization owns the land, and access requires a permit obtainable at Ghost Ranch — but it is free and straightforward. [3]

Plaza Blanca is one of those places that photographs do not prepare you for. The white rock absorbs and reflects light in a way that makes it look illuminated from within on overcast days. Spend half an hour in there and you understand why O'Keeffe kept coming back.

Genízaros in colonial New Mexico — New Mexico Office of the State Historian
Plaza Blanca access — Ghost Ranch

name: Hernandez — Ansel Adams & Moonrise
body: The small village of Hernandez is coming up on your left, and if you know one photograph by Ansel Adams, there's a reasonable chance it was taken right around here.

On October 31, 1941, Adams was driving south on US-84 — the same road you're on — returning from a trip through the Chama Valley with his assistant. The sun was low in the southwest. The moon, nearly full, was rising in the east over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. The white crosses of the Hernandez cemetery were catching the last of the direct sunlight against a sky that was darkening in the east and still lit in the west. Adams pulled over, scrambled to set up his large-format camera, and made one exposure before the light was gone. He didn't have his exposure meter and calculated the exposure from memory, using the known luminance of the full moon. [1]

The resulting photograph — 'Moonrise, Hernandez, New Mexico' — became one of the most reproduced and most valuable photographs in the history of the medium. Adams printed it hundreds of times over the following decades, refining the printing each time, making the sky progressively darker and the crosses progressively brighter until the image reached the high-contrast drama of the well-known prints. [2] Original prints from the 1940s are lighter and cooler in tone than the later versions — different photographs, in a sense, made from the same negative.

The cemetery is still there, though the exact viewpoint Adams used is uncertain and the landscape around it has changed enough that the precise angle is no longer reproducible. You can see the village, the mountains in the east, and if the timing is right, a similar light. What you can't replicate is the combination of 40 years of practiced vision, a large-format camera, and the luck of being on the right road at the right moment.

sources.text: Moonrise, Hernandez, New Mexico: circumstances of capture — Ansel Adams Gallery
Moonrise, Hernandez, New Mexico — Wikipedia

name: Ohkay Owingeh & the 1680 Pueblo Revolt
body: The pueblo visible to your right, across the Rio Grande, is Ohkay Owingeh — which means 'place of the strong people' in Tewa. The Spanish called it San Juan Pueblo, and the federal government used that name in official records until the pueblo formally reclaimed its traditional name in 2005. [1] It is one of the northernmost of the Rio Grande Pueblos, and it is, by one reasonable measure, the most consequential Native American community in American history.

The first Spanish colonial capital of New Mexico was established here in 1598, when Don Juan de Oñate led a colonizing expedition north from Mexico and established his government in the existing Pueblo of Ohkay Owingeh — simply moving in, displacing the residents to the adjacent village, and using their homes and food stores as the foundation of the colony. [2] It was an inauspicious beginning to a relationship that deteriorated steadily over the following 80 years.

The 1680 Pueblo Revolt began here — or more precisely, it was organized here, in secret, by a religious leader named Popé. Popé was a Tewa man from Ohkay Owingeh who had been arrested by the Spanish in 1675 along with 46 other Pueblo religious leaders, accused of sorcery — meaning, of continuing to practice traditional religion in defiance of the Catholic missions. They were flogged and three were hanged. [3] Popé returned home furious and spent the following five years building a coalition.

The logistics of the revolt were extraordinary. Popé organized 17,000 Pueblo people across dozens of villages speaking six different languages, spread over 400 miles of the Rio Grande valley — communities that had histories of conflict with each other, that had competed for resources and raided each other before the Spanish arrived. He convinced them all to act simultaneously, on a single date, before the Spanish had any warning.

The original date was August 11, 1680. When the Spanish learned of the plan two days before — two Pueblo men from Tesuque told the governor of the conspiracy — Popé moved the date up to August 10. On the morning of August 10, 1680, the Pueblo people rose up simultaneously from Taos in the north to Isleta in the south. They killed 21 of 33 Franciscan priests in New Mexico. They killed approximately 400 of the 2,500 Spanish colonists. They besieged the surviving colonists in Santa Fe for several days before Governor Otermín retreated south through the Jornada del Muerto with the survivors. [4]

For the next 12 years, New Mexico was governed by the Pueblo people. The kivas that the Spanish had ordered destroyed were rebuilt. The traditional ceremonies that the missions had suppressed were restored. The Spanish returned in 1692 under Diego de Vargas, and the reconquest was complex — some pueblos welcomed the Spanish back, others resisted, there was further violence. But the terms were different after 1680. The Spanish never again forced Pueblo people to perform unpaid labor. They never again attempted to suppress traditional religious practices. [5]

Popé died around 1690, before the reconquest. He is buried somewhere in the ruins of Ohkay Owingeh, though the exact location is unknown. In 2005, the state of New Mexico placed a bronze statue of Popé in the National Statuary Hall of the U.S. Capitol — one of the two figures New Mexico selected to represent the state. [6] He stands in the Capitol building in Washington, 400 years after organizing the most successful Indigenous uprising in American history.

Pueblo Revolt of 1680 — Wikipedia
After the Revolt: Spanish reconquest and changed terms — Britannica

name: Española — Lowrider Capital of the World
body: You're passing through Española, and the self-proclaimed title on the water tower says it all: Lowrider Capital of the World. This is not mere boosterism. Northern New Mexico's lowrider tradition is genuine, deep-rooted, and artistically serious.

The lowrider emerged from the Mexican-American communities of Southern California in the 1940s and 1950s — a deliberate counter-aesthetic to the customizing culture that prized speed and power. A lowrider moves slowly, deliberately, close to the ground, asserting presence and pride in space that was not always welcoming. By the 1960s and 1970s, the tradition had taken root in northern New Mexico, where the combination of mechanical aptitude, available older vehicles, and a strong tradition of decorative arts — santos, retablos, embroidered textiles — found expression in a new form. [1]

Northern New Mexico lowriders are often distinguished by their elaborate custom paint work and interior decoration, which draws on the Hispano artistic tradition in ways that set them apart from the California lowrider aesthetic. Gold leaf lettering, sacred heart imagery, Virgin of Guadalupe portraits, landscape murals — the cars function as rolling altarpieces, combining automotive craft with devotional art in a way that makes sense in a region where the church, the community, and artistic expression have been intertwined for four centuries. [2]

The Chimayó Museum and the New Mexico History Museum in Santa Fe have both mounted serious exhibitions on lowrider culture, and the vehicles that appear at show circuits in northern New Mexico are the product of decades of work by individual craftspeople. If you see a lowrider on the road here, look closely — the paint work alone may represent hundreds of hours.

sources.text: Lowrider culture: history and origins — Wikipedia
New Mexico lowriders and Hispano artistic tradition — New Mexico History Museum

name: El Rancho de las Golondrinas
body: El Rancho de las Golondrinas — the Ranch of the Swallows — sits just off the highway south of Santa Fe, and it is the best-preserved example of a Spanish colonial rancho in North America. The name refers to the cliff swallows that nest in the acequia banks and the adobe walls of the property every spring.

The rancho occupies a critical spot in the colonial geography of New Mexico. It was the last paraje — resting place — on El Camino Real before Santa Fe. Travelers who had survived the Jornada del Muerto, crossed the Río Grande, and made their way north through the Mesilla Valley and the Socorro basin would arrive at Las Golondrinas for a final rest and resupply before entering the capital. The property has been documented as a stopping place since the early 18th century. [1]

The current operation is a living history museum covering about 200 acres, maintained by a nonprofit that has spent decades preserving and restoring the original structures and adding relocated historic buildings from throughout the region. The compound includes a defensive tower, a water-powered grist mill still in working order, a threshing floor, a blacksmith shop, a winery with a functioning grape press, multiple chapels, and about a dozen replanted historic gardens with the medicinal and culinary plants of the colonial period. [2]

During open weekends from spring through fall, costumed interpreters work throughout the compound in character — grinding corn, weaving on period looms, firing the hornos, pressing grapes. It has the feel of a place that is actually used rather than merely preserved. The acequia that runs through the property is the same acequia the original ranchers dug, and it still irrigates the fields in the summer.

The spring and fall festivals at Las Golondrinas are worth planning around if your dates align. The Harvest Festival in late September or early October involves traditional food production — the molasses mill, the grape pressing, the chile roasting — that is genuinely participatory rather than merely observed.

Even on a regular open day without a festival, Las Golondrinas rewards an hour or two. It's one of the few places in the Southwest where you can understand, physically and sensory, what the Spanish colonial agricultural economy actually looked and smelled and sounded like.

sources.text: El Rancho de las Golondrinas: history and Camino Real significance — Golondrinas official
El Rancho de las Golondrinas living history museum — Wikipedia

name: Black Mesa
body: The flat-topped black volcanic mesa rising above the Rio Grande on your left is Black Mesa — Tunyo in Tewa — and it is one of the most sacred sites in the northern Rio Grande Pueblo world.

Black Mesa is a lava flow of relatively recent origin — geologically speaking, the volcanism that created it occurred within the last few million years, part of the Jemez volcanic field activity. The basalt cap that gives the mesa its dark color and its characteristic flat profile is hard enough to resist erosion while the lighter sedimentary rock around and below it has been carved away by the river. The result is an isolated, visually commanding platform rising about 400 feet above the surrounding terrain. [1]

The significance of Black Mesa to San Ildefonso Pueblo, which sits at its base, extends far back into pre-contact history. The mesa appears in Tewa creation narratives and in the oral history of conflicts between the Pueblo people and their enemies. It served as a defensive refuge — the steep basalt faces on all sides made it nearly impregnable. During the period after the 1680 Pueblo Revolt, when Spanish forces were attempting to reconquer New Mexico, the people of San Ildefonso held out on Black Mesa for months before negotiating terms with the Spanish. [2]

San Ildefonso Pueblo is also known as the home of Maria Martinez, arguably the most celebrated Native American artist of the 20th century. Martinez revived and transformed the tradition of black-on-black burnished pottery in the 1910s and 1920s, collaborating with her husband Julian on the distinctive matte-on-polished-black work that is now among the most collected Pueblo ceramics in the world. [3] The San Ildefonso Pueblo Museum and the Black Mesa serve together as the context for that artistic legacy.

sources.text: Black Mesa geology and Jemez volcanic field — USGS
San Ildefonso Pueblo and Black Mesa during the Pueblo Revolt — Wikipedia
Maria Martinez: black-on-black pottery tradition — Smithsonian American Art Museum

name: Camel Rock & Tesuque Pueblo
body: The distinctive rock formation on your right — the one that does in fact look remarkably like a seated camel — is Camel Rock, on the land of Tesuque Pueblo.

Tesuque is the southernmost of the Tewa-speaking pueblos, sitting just north of Santa Fe at the edge of the Sangre de Cristo foothills. The Tesuque people have occupied this area since at least the 14th century, and the pueblo has the particular historical significance of being the first community to rise in the 1680 Pueblo Revolt — Tesuque warriors killed the priest sent to warn them of the uprising and struck their first blows before dawn on August 10, giving the revolt its irreversible momentum before Popé's coordinating signal had even reached most of the other pueblos. [1]

The pueblo today is small — a few hundred enrolled members — but economically active. The Camel Rock Casino on the highway is the primary employer, and the combination of gaming revenue and proximity to Santa Fe has given the pueblo more economic stability than many of the more isolated northern communities. [2]

The rock itself has been eroding continuously since anyone has been looking at it. The camel's nose is noticeably shorter in recent photographs than in images from the mid-20th century. At some point in the geologically near future, the overhanging rock will collapse and the camel will cease to exist. For now, it's still remarkably convincing.

sources.text: Tesuque Pueblo and the 1680 Pueblo Revolt — Wikipedia
Tesuque Pueblo government and economy — Pueblo of Tesuque official

name: Cerrillos Hills & Turquoise Mines
body: The low volcanic hills to your east — the Cerrillos Hills — are one of the oldest continuously mined areas in North America, and the reason is turquoise.

The Cerrillos Hills contain several distinct turquoise deposits formed by hydrothermal processes in the volcanic rock, and Pueblo people have been mining them for at least 1,500 years, possibly longer. The scale of the prehistoric mining operation was staggering: archaeologists estimate that between 100,000 and a billion pounds of rock were removed from the Cerrillos mines over the prehistoric period, using stone tools, fire-setting, and simple wooden supports. [1] The turquoise from these mines moved through trade networks extending from the Pacific Coast to the Mississippi Valley and deep into Mexico — Cerrillos turquoise has been identified in Chacoan great houses hundreds of miles away and in Aztec archaeological sites in central Mexico. [2]

Turquoise in the Pueblo world was not primarily decorative. It was cosmologically significant — associated with water, the sky, and the spiritual power of certain beings. Its value was religious as well as economic, and controlling access to turquoise sources was a form of political and ritual power. The concentration of mining at Cerrillos may explain some of the political organization of the prehistoric Southwest: whoever controlled the Cerrillos Hills controlled access to a resource that the entire region wanted. [3]

The Spanish mined the Cerrillos Hills for lead, silver, and zinc in the colonial period, and American miners came for the same metals in the 19th century. The town of Cerrillos — which you'll pass in a moment — was a boom town in the 1880s with multiple saloons, a newspaper, and ambitions of becoming the capital of New Mexico. The railroad missed it by a few miles, and the boom collapsed. It has been a quiet village ever since, with a turquoise mining park that offers tours and lets visitors dig for their own specimens. [4]

name: Madrid — Ghost Town & Artist Colony
body: Madrid — pronounced MAD-rid by locals, a point of regional pride that distinguishes it from the Spanish capital — was a coal mining town that Albuquerque Gas and Electric built from scratch in the early 20th century to supply fuel for its power plants. At its peak in the 1920s, Madrid had a population of around 2,500 people, a company-owned company store, a ballpark that hosted games against teams from Albuquerque and Santa Fe, and a Christmas light display so elaborate that the town advertised itself as a tourist destination and planes flew over at night to see it. [1]

The Albuquerque transit to natural gas in the late 1940s made the coal mines uneconomical almost overnight. The company closed the mines in 1954 and the town was abandoned — all 150 houses, the company store, the mine buildings, the baseball diamond, all of it standing empty. The Huber family, which owned the property, declined to demolish the structures. Madrid sat vacant for two decades. [2]

In the early 1970s, artists and back-to-the-land settlers discovered it. The Hubers began renting the empty houses for next to nothing, and a community of musicians, craftspeople, and counterculture refugees took up residence in the abandoned coal town. Madrid became, gradually and organically, an arts colony without any planning or institutional support — just cheap real estate, good light, and a critical mass of people who made things. [3]

The town today has galleries, studios, restaurants, and the Mine Shaft Tavern, which has been operating continuously since the 1970s and hosts live music on weekends. It attracts enough tourists on weekend days that parking can be challenging, but on a weekday morning it retains something of the ramshackle, unfinished quality that makes it genuinely interesting. The original mining infrastructure is still visible in the hills above town.

name: Route 66 Musical Highway
body: About a mile ahead on the old Route 66 alignment — if you're on NM-333 rather than I-40 through the Tijeras Canyon — is the Musical Highway, a stretch of road designed to play 'America the Beautiful' as you drive over it at the correct speed.

The system works through precisely spaced grooves cut into the asphalt — the same principle as a rumble strip, but patterned to produce specific notes rather than undifferentiated vibration. The New Mexico Department of Transportation installed it in 2014 as a tourism gimmick tied to Route 66 nostalgia. The correct speed is 45 miles per hour; too fast and the pitch rises, too slow and it drops. [1]

The original installation was placed in the wrong location — too close to a residential neighborhood — and had to be moved after residents complained about the noise from passing cars. The relocated version is on a less residential stretch and still functions, though the quality of the 'music' depends heavily on tire width, vehicle weight, and your willingness to hear a melody in something that mostly sounds like a loud road. [2]

The real Route 66 nostalgia on this stretch is the Tijeras Canyon itself — the pass through the Sandia and Manzano Mountains that was the only practical eastern approach to Albuquerque before the interstate was built. Driving it now, on the old two-lane alignment, gives you a physical sense of what cross-country driving actually felt like in the era when Route 66 was the main artery between Chicago and Los Angeles.

sources.text: New Mexico Musical Highway installation, 2014 — New Mexico DOT
Route 66 Musical Highway, Tijeras — Wikipedia

name: Acoma Sky City
body: About 60 miles west of Albuquerque, visible from I-40 as a flat-topped mesa rising 367 feet above the surrounding plateau, is Acoma Pueblo — Sky City. And if there is one place on this entire trip that most rewards a stop, it may be this one.

Acoma has been continuously inhabited since at least 1150 CE, and possibly as early as 800 CE — making it one of the oldest continuously occupied communities in North America. [1] The mesa top was chosen for the same reason high ground has always been chosen: it was defensible. The surrounding plateau drops away sharply on all sides, the hand-and-toe-hold trails cut into the sandstone are the only original access points, and an approaching enemy could be seen from miles away.

The pueblo on top of the mesa is extraordinary. The narrow streets between the multistory adobe buildings are not arranged on a grid — they follow the irregular form of the mesa top, turning and narrowing in ways that force visitors to slow down. The houses are the same buff-colored sandstone as the mesa beneath them, so that the buildings seem to grow from the rock rather than stand on it. The total area of the mesa top is about 70 acres.

The Spanish first reached Acoma in 1540, during Coronado's expedition. The encounter was tense, and when a Spanish captain and several soldiers were killed at Acoma in 1598 during a dispute over provisions, the response was catastrophic. Juan de Oñate sent a punitive force under his nephew Vicente de Zaldívar. In three days of fighting in January 1599, Spanish forces killed approximately 800 Acoma people. About 500 survivors were captured and sentenced: men over 25 years old had one foot amputated and served 20 years of personal servitude. Women over 12 served 20 years of servitude. Children were separated from their families and given to the Spanish as servants. [2]

This is documented. Oñate's trial records, his orders, and the accounts of the battle survive in the Spanish colonial archive and have been translated and published. Oñate was eventually convicted by the Spanish colonial courts of multiple offenses, including excessive cruelty to the Indians, and was banned from New Mexico. The conviction came too late for the 800 dead. [3]

The Acoma people rebuilt. The mission church of San Esteban del Rey, which stands at the edge of the mesa top, was built between 1629 and 1640 using forced Acoma labor. The building materials — massive vigas of ponderosa pine, stone, adobe — were carried up the 367-foot trail by hand. The church walls are 60 feet high and 10 feet thick. [4] It is one of the most impressive buildings in New Mexico and one of the most troubling, given what it cost to build.

Acoma is a sovereign tribal nation, and access to the mesa top is by guided tour only, departing from the Sky City Cultural Center and Haak'u Museum at the base of the mesa. The museum is genuinely excellent — it covers Acoma history, culture, and art with depth and on Acoma's own terms, which means you get the Oñate story and the amputation sentences and all of it, not a sanitized version. The pottery tradition at Acoma — thin-walled white-slipped ware with fine geometric designs in black, orange, and red — is among the most technically accomplished in the Pueblo world, and the museum collection illustrates why. [5]

The tour guides are Acoma community members, and the quality of the experience depends significantly on the guide you get — some are extraordinary storytellers. Bring water, sunscreen, and appropriate footwear. The mesa top is exposed and the summer sun is relentless.

name: Rio Puerco & the High Desert Return
body: You're on the return now, heading east on I-40 through the high desert west of Albuquerque, and the landscape you're driving through is a good subject for the last extended narration of this trip.

The Rio Puerco — the 'dirty river,' named for its high sediment load — crosses I-40 about 15 miles west of Albuquerque. It's usually dry or nearly so at the road crossing, but the drainage it occupies is significant: the Puerco is the largest tributary of the Rio Grande in New Mexico, draining a vast area of the Colorado Plateau and the high desert of western New Mexico. When it runs, it carries enormous amounts of sediment — the clay-rich soils of the watershed make the water a characteristic red-brown color. [1]

The mesa country through here is part of the southern edge of the Colorado Plateau — the massive geological province that covers much of the Four Corners region and extends east to the Rio Grande Rift. The flat-topped mesas are made of resistant sandstone and limestone that cap softer shales below; as the shales erode, the cap rocks are left standing. The Cañoncito Navajo community — formally the Tohajiilee Chapter of the Navajo Nation — has land on both sides of I-40 through this area, a reminder that the highway you're on runs through territory that has been continuously Native American land for centuries. [2]

The West Mesa Escarpment you can see to your right — that long, dark, volcanic ridge — is a string of shield volcanoes that erupted roughly 150,000 years ago and created the basalt flows that form the mesa edge of modern Albuquerque. The volcanoes are visible as rounded cones along the escarpment. They're part of the same Rio Grande Rift volcanic system that created the Jemez caldera, the Taos Plateau flows, and the malpais lava fields around Grants to the west. [3]

As you approach Albuquerque from the west, the Sandia Mountains come into view to the east — the long wall of pink granite rising nearly 11,000 feet above the city. 'Sandia' means 'watermelon' in Spanish, and at sunset the granite turns a deep pink that the name is supposed to explain. The range rises more than a mile above the city — the vertical relief is comparable to the front face of the Colorado Rockies. The Sandia Tramway, at 2.7 miles the longest aerial tramway in North America, runs from the northeast foothills to the summit crest, and the temperature at the top is typically 25 to 30 degrees cooler than at the base. [4]

This return drive brings the full arc of the trip into view. You've been in desert at 3,700 feet elevation and forest at 8,000 feet. You've seen geological time measured in hundreds of millions of years and human time measured in continuous centuries of occupation. You've passed through the territories of at least two dozen Native nations, through the colonial geography of three successive empires, through the science of nuclear weapons and the art of bone paintings and the engineering of dams that moved rivers.

New Mexico is a place that rewards this kind of attention — the slow drive, the willingness to pull over, the curiosity about why things are where they are. The desert doesn't give itself up quickly. Neither does the history. But both are extraordinarily deep once you start pulling on the thread.
